proposals.title: Deactivation Mechanisms and Long-Term Stability of Pd-Based Bimetallic Catalysts for the Selective Oxidation of Methane via In Situ H₂O₂
proposals.motivation: The selective oxidation of methane to methanol using in situ generated H₂O₂ is a promising route for methane valorization, but its industrial viability depends on catalyst longevity. Pre-2026 work by Hutchings and others has demonstrated promising initial activity with Pd-based bimetallic systems, yet their stability over extended operation and the fundamental causes of deactivation remain poorly understood. A systematic study of recyclability limits is critical to identify practical bottlenecks and guide the design of robust catalysts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the primary deactivation mechanisms for Pd-Au/TiO₂ (rutile) catalysts in this reaction are not sintering, but rather involve chloride-induced leaching of the active metal and the formation of carbonaceous deposits, which are exacerbated by the unique local environment created during in situ H₂O₂ synthesis.
proposals.method: We will subject state-of-the-art Pd-Au/TiO₂ (rutile) catalysts to prolonged, multi-cycle testing under standard methane oxidation conditions with in situ H₂O₂ generation. Post-reaction characterization will be conducted after defined intervals using HAADF-STEM/EDX to track nanoparticle size, composition, and dispersion. XPS and TPO will be used to quantify surface chloride retention and carbon deposition. Controlled experiments will systematically vary the concentration of dissolved chlorides (e.g., HCl, NaCl) in the aqueous medium to isolate their specific influence on metal leaching and catalyst structure, linking these changes directly to activity and selectivity decay.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed mechanistic map of deactivation pathways for this promising catalytic system, moving beyond initial activity reports. It will establish clear structure-activity-stability relationships and identify the most detrimental factors—be it chloride poisoning, coking, or metal leaching—limiting catalyst life. The findings will deliver actionable guidelines for designing more durable catalysts and optimizing reaction conditions for sustained performance.
proposals.novelty: While catalyst deactivation is a general concern, the specific interplay between in situ H₂O₂ synthesis, dissolved chlorides (a common component from precursor salts), and the stability of bimetallic nanoparticles in aqueous methane oxidation has not been systematically investigated, representing a critical and unexplored gap for this reaction.

proposals.title: Operando Structural Evolution of Pd-Au/TiO2 Catalysts During Methane Oxidation: The Influence of Aqueous Chlorides on Active Site Integrity
proposals.motivation: Hutchings' recent work demonstrates that in situ H2O2 synthesis over Pd-based bimetallics enables selective methane oxidation, while parallel studies show dissolved chlorides profoundly influence titania's photocatalytic properties. Catalysts are typically characterized ex situ, but the aggressive, halide-containing reaction medium may dynamically alter the solid-state structure, leading to a disconnect between pre-catalytic characterization and the true active phase. Understanding this is critical for designing stable, selective catalysts for methane valorization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the bimetallic structure and oxidation state of Pd-Au nanoparticles supported on rutile TiO2, characterized ex situ, undergo significant and reversible restructuring under operando conditions in an aqueous, chloride-containing medium during methane oxidation, and that these dynamic changes dictate catalytic performance.
proposals.method: We will synthesize Pd-Au catalysts on rutile TiO2, characterized pre-reaction by XRD, XPS, and HAADF-STEM. Operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) at the Pd K-edge and Au L3-edge will be performed during catalytic testing in a high-pressure batch reactor, using a reaction mixture of CH4, O2, H2, and HCl to simulate in situ H2O2 generation in chloride media. Parallel quasi-in situ XPS analysis of catalysts rapidly transferred from the reaction will correlate electronic states with XAFS-derived structural metrics. The influence of chloride concentration and reaction time on structural parameters (coordination numbers, oxidation states) will be quantitatively tracked.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly link dynamic, medium-induced structural changes in bimetallic catalysts to their activity and selectivity for methane oxidation, moving beyond static characterization. It will provide a mechanistic framework for understanding catalyst deactivation or promotion by halides, offering new design principles for catalysts operating in complex, aqueous environments.
proposals.novelty: While operando studies of catalysts are established, applying this to probe the stability of precisely engineered bimetallic structures in the context of in situ H2O2 synthesis and chloride effects is novel, merging two distinct strands of Hutchings' pre-2026 research into a unified, dynamic investigation.

proposals.title: Unveiling Active Site Dynamics and Speciation in Ultra-Low Loading Pd-Based Bimetallic Catalysts for In Situ H2O2 Synthesis and Methane Oxidation
proposals.motivation: Industrial catalytic processes often operate with extremely low metal loadings (<0.1 wt%) for economic and environmental reasons, yet fundamental studies typically use high-loading model systems. This gap obscures the true nature of the active sites under practical conditions, where speciation, stability, and the influence of promoters or contaminants like chlorides may differ dramatically. Understanding catalyst behavior in these ultra-low concentration regimes is critical for rational design and optimization of processes like the selective oxidation of methane to methanol via in situ H2O2 synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that at ultra-low metal loadings (<0.05 wt% Pd) on rutile TiO2, the dominant active species for in situ H2O2 generation shifts from nanoparticles to highly dispersed, cationic Pd ensembles, and that trace chloride ions, common in industrial water streams, will selectively poison or modify these specific sites, thereby drastically altering the methane oxidation pathway.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of Pd-Au and Pd-Cu bimetallic catalysts on rutile TiO2 with total metal loadings from 0.01 to 0.5 wt% using strong electrostatic adsorption. Catalytic testing will involve in situ H2O2 synthesis from H2 and O2 followed by methane oxidation in a continuous flow reactor. Advanced characterization will employ in situ/operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) and diffuse reflectance UV-Vis to track metal speciation and oxidation state under reaction conditions. The influence of dissolved chlorides (1-100 ppm) will be systematically studied by introducing NaCl into the aqueous reaction medium, correlating changes in activity with spectroscopic signatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, mechanistic link between ultra-low loading catalyst structure and function for a key green chemistry reaction. It will establish how trace chloride impurities, a major practical concern, interact with distinct catalytic sites, offering guidelines for catalyst design in non-ideal media. The findings will bridge the gap between high-loading model studies and industrial catalyst operation.
proposals.novelty: While Hutchings' group has pioneered bimetallic catalysts for this reaction, the explicit focus on the sub-0.1 wt% loading regime combined with operando speciation studies under chloride influence is unexplored. This moves beyond typical activity comparisons to deconvolute the active site identity and its vulnerability to poisons at industrially relevant concentrations.

proposals.title: Single-Site Copper Catalysts on Chloride-Modified Rutile TiO2 for the Selective Oxidation of Methane to Methanol via In Situ H2O2
proposals.motivation: The selective partial oxidation of methane to methanol remains a major challenge in catalysis. While recent work, including from Hutchings' group, shows promise using in situ H2O2 over Pd-based bimetallics, reliance on noble metals limits scalability. Developing high-selectivity catalysts from earth-abundant, single metals would dramatically improve the economic viability of direct methane valorization.
proposals.hypothesis: That isolated, single-site Cu(II) species, anchored on a chloride-modified rutile TiO2 support, can catalyze the in situ generation of H2O2 and its subsequent use for methane oxidation with selectivity rivaling noble-metal alloys, by creating a highly electrophilic site that mimics the function of bimetallic Pd centers.
proposals.method: We will synthesize catalysts via strong electrostatic adsorption of Cu ammine complexes onto high-surface-area rutile TiO2, followed by calcination to create isolated CuOx sites. The rutile support will be pre-treated with aqueous HCl to introduce surface chlorides, known to influence TiO2 surface properties and metal anchoring. Catalytic testing will follow established protocols for methane oxidation with H2 and O2, using a fixed-bed reactor with online GC analysis. Advanced characterization (HAADF-STEM, XAS, in situ DRIFTS) will correlate Cu speciation and chloride presence with H2O2 yield and methanol selectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a design principle for creating highly selective, noble-metal-free oxidation catalysts by engineering single-site geometries on tailored oxide supports. It will directly challenge the paradigm that high selectivity requires bimetallic noble metal systems, providing a cheaper, sustainable alternative for methane upgrading.
proposals.novelty: The combination of a single, non-noble metal (Cu) with a chloride-modified rutile support for this reaction is unexplored. The novelty lies in using chloride not just as a modifier of TiO2 photocatalysis, but as a critical ligand to stabilize the active Cu site for selective thermocatalytic methane oxidation via the in situ peroxide pathway.

proposals.title: Continuous-Flow Methane Oxidation to Methanol: Assessing the Long-Term Stability and Chloride Tolerance of Pd-Sn Bimetallic Catalysts
proposals.motivation: The direct, selective oxidation of methane to methanol remains a critical challenge in catalysis. While recent work, including from our group, has shown promise using in situ H₂O₂ generation over Pd-based bimetallics like Pd-Sn, these studies are predominantly conducted in batch reactors. For industrial viability, catalyst performance and stability must be evaluated under continuous flow conditions, which better mimic process operations and can induce different deactivation mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Pd-Sn bimetallic catalysts, which show high selectivity in batch studies, will exhibit superior long-term stability and resistance to chloride-induced deactivation in a continuous flow reactor compared to monometallic Pd, due to the electronic and structural influence of Sn.
proposals.method: We will synthesize Pd-Sn catalysts supported on TiO₂ (rutile), a support known to influence catalytic properties in oxidation. These will be tested in a fixed-bed continuous flow reactor for the methane-to-methanol reaction using in situ generated H₂O₂ (from H₂ and O₂). The reaction conditions (pressure, temperature, gas hourly space velocity) will be optimized. Long-term stability tests (>100 hours) will be conducted, with deliberate introduction of chloride ions (as HCl or NaCl) into the aqueous phase to study their influence on catalyst deactivation, linking to known effects of chlorides on titania surfaces. Performance will be monitored via online GC and HPLC.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed assessment of Pd-Sn bimetallic catalysts under industrially relevant continuous flow conditions for methane oxidation. It will deliver critical data on long-term stability and identify specific deactivation pathways, particularly related to chloride poisoning, bridging the gap between promising batch chemistry and potential process implementation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging the investigation of promising Pd-Sn bimetallic catalysts with rigorous continuous-flow testing and a targeted study on chloride poisoning, a key practical concern often overlooked in fundamental batch studies of this reaction.

proposals.title: Enhanced Methane to Methanol via In Situ H2O2 over Pd-Sn Nanoparticles Supported on High-Surface-Area Rutile TiO2: Influence of Chloride Residues
proposals.motivation: The selective oxidation of methane to methanol using in situ generated H2O2 is a critical challenge in catalysis, offering a route to valorize natural gas. Pre-2026 work by Hutchings and others shows that Pd-Sn bimetallic catalysts are promising, but their practical application is limited by inefficient metal use and potential deactivation from chloride ions, a common impurity in water and waste streams. This project addresses the need for robust, high-activity catalysts by engineering the support and understanding environmental poisons.
proposals.hypothesis: Depositing Pd-Sn nanoparticles on a high-surface-area rutile TiO2 support will significantly enhance catalytic activity for methane oxidation by improving H2O2 generation efficiency and metal dispersion, but residual chloride ions will competitively adsorb and influence the oxidation properties of the titania surface.
proposals.method: High-surface-area rutile TiO2 will be synthesized via hydrothermal methods. Pd-Sn nanoparticles will be deposited via colloidal or wet impregnation techniques, with careful control of Sn/Pd ratios. Catalysts will be characterized using XRD, TEM, XPS, and H2 chemisorption. Catalytic testing for methane oxidation with in situ H2O2 synthesis will be performed in a fixed-bed reactor. The influence of deliberately added chlorides (e.g., NaCl) on reaction rate, selectivity, and catalyst stability will be systematically studied, linking to prior work on chloride effects in photocatalytic titania systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish structure-activity relationships for supported Pd-Sn catalysts, directly translating fundamental bimetallic concepts into a practical, high-surface-area material. It will provide crucial insights into the poisoning mechanism of chlorides on precious metal-titania systems for oxidation catalysis, guiding the design of catalysts for real-world, non-ideal feedstocks.
proposals.novelty: The combination of a high-surface-area rutile support (leveraging its known stability) with a tailored Pd-Sn bimetallic phase for this reaction is unexplored. Explicitly connecting the study of chloride influence from photocatalytic wastewater treatment to a targeted, thermocatalytic methane oxidation system represents a novel cross-disciplinary investigation of catalyst deactivation.

proposals.title: H2O2 as a Synthesis and Regeneration Tool for Pd-Sn/TiO2 Catalysts: Enhancing Activity and Stability for Methane Oxidation
proposals.motivation: The selective oxidation of methane to methanol using in situ generated H2O2 is a major challenge in catalysis. While Pd-Sn bimetallic systems show promise, catalyst deactivation remains a significant barrier. Concurrently, Hutchings' group has shown that dissolved chlorides influence the surface properties of titania, a common support. This proposal addresses catalyst longevity by using H2O2 not just as a reactant, but as a tool to engineer the catalyst itself.
proposals.hypothesis: Using H2O2 as an oxidant during the synthesis or periodic regeneration of Pd-Sn catalysts will create and maintain a more active and stable SnOx-Pd interface on a TiO2 (rutile) support, leading to sustained performance in the selective oxidation of methane to methanol.
proposals.method: We will synthesize Pd-Sn catalysts via wet impregnation on a rutile TiO2 support. The key intervention is the treatment of the fresh or spent catalyst with dilute H2O2 solutions under controlled conditions (temperature, concentration, time). Catalysts will be characterized using XRD, XPS, TEM, and H2-TPR to analyze Sn oxidation state, Pd dispersion, and metal-support interactions. Their catalytic performance will be evaluated in a fixed-bed reactor for methane oxidation with in situ H2O2 generation (H2/O2 mix), comparing activity, methanol selectivity, and lifetime against conventionally prepared and regenerated (e.g., H2 reduction) catalysts.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel methodology for catalyst synthesis/regeneration using the target oxidant (H2O2), directly linking material engineering to reaction mechanism. It will provide fundamental insights into how controlled surface oxidation stabilizes bimetallic structures. The findings could lead to more durable and efficient catalysts for direct methane valorization.
proposals.novelty: While H2O2 is well-studied as a co-reactant in methane oxidation, its deliberate use as a synthesis and regeneration agent for the bimetallic catalyst is unexplored. This approach inverts the typical paradigm, using the product to engineer the active site, and integrates insights from chloride-influenced TiO2 surface chemistry.

proposals.title: Selective Catalytic Ring-Opening Oxidation of Aromatics via In Situ H2O2 over Non-Noble Metal Rutile Catalysts: The Influence of Chloride Promotion
proposals.motivation: The selective oxidation of inert C-H bonds, such as in methane or aromatic rings, remains a grand challenge in catalysis. While recent work, including by Hutchings' group, demonstrates the efficacy of in situ H2O2 generation over Pd-based catalysts for methane-to-methanol, the reliance on noble metals limits scalability and economic viability. Extending this powerful strategy to more complex, yet industrially relevant, aromatic substrates with earth-abundant catalysts could revolutionize fine chemical synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tailored non-noble metal oxides, specifically rutile-phase TiO2 modified with transition metals (e.g., Fe, Cu, Co), can catalyze the selective ring-opening oxidation of model aromatics (e.g., benzene to phenol/quinones) using in situ generated H2O2, and that the addition of chloride ions will significantly enhance both activity and selectivity by modulating surface properties and peroxide activation pathways.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of M-doped rutile TiO2 catalysts (M = Fe, Cu, Co, Mn) via hydrothermal and co-precipitation methods, rigorously characterizing them using XRD, XPS, and TEM. Catalytic testing will involve the concurrent reaction of H2 and O2 to generate H2O2 in situ over the catalyst in an aqueous medium containing the aromatic substrate and controlled concentrations of alkali chlorides (e.g., NaCl, KCl). Activity (conversion) and selectivity (to oxygenated ring-opened products) will be quantified via GC-MS and HPLC, with systematic variation of chloride concentration, pH, and transition metal dopant identity to establish structure-activity relationships.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new family of chloride-promoted, non-noble metal catalysts for selective oxidative C-C bond cleavage, directly challenging the necessity of Pd or Au for such reactions. It will provide fundamental insights into the role of chlorides in modulating the redox properties of rutile surfaces and the mechanism of H2O2 activation for selective oxidation, bridging concepts from photocatalytic wastewater treatment and thermocatalysis.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of the in situ H2O2 strategy—previously confined to noble-metal methane oxidation—with the known influence of chlorides on titania properties to drive selective ring-opening of aromatics using base-metal catalysts is a significant and non-obvious advance beyond the current state-of-the-art.

proposals.title: Visible-Light Photocatalytic Epoxidation of Propylene Using In Situ Generated H2O2 over Chloride-Modified Rutile TiO2 Catalysts
proposals.motivation: The epoxidation of light alkenes like propylene to form epoxides is a critical industrial process, but current routes often require harsh conditions and costly oxidants. Hutchings' recent work demonstrates the potential of in situ H2O2 synthesis for selective methane oxidation. Transferring this concept to alkene epoxidation under mild, photocatalytic conditions could enable more sustainable and energy-efficient chemical synthesis, reducing reliance on aggressive reagents and high temperatures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rutile-phase TiO2, known for its stability and photocatalytic properties, can be modified with surface chlorides to create a bifunctional catalyst that both generates H2O2 from H2 and O2 under visible light and uses the in situ oxidant to drive the selective epoxidation of propylene to propylene oxide at ambient temperature and pressure.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of rutile TiO2 catalysts via hydrothermal methods and modify their surfaces with controlled amounts of chloride anions through post-synthetic treatments or co-precipitation. These materials will be tested in a continuous-flow photocatalytic reactor under visible-light irradiation (λ > 420 nm) using a feed of C3H6, H2, and O2. The influence of chloride loading, rutile morphology, and reaction conditions (light intensity, flow rate) on activity and selectivity will be systematically evaluated using gas chromatography, UV-Vis spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy to correlate surface properties with performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, mild photocatalytic route for alkene epoxidation by merging the concepts of in situ oxidant generation and semiconductor photocatalysis. It will provide fundamental insights into how chloride anions influence both the photocatalytic generation of H2O2 and its subsequent use in selective oxidation on a metal-oxide surface, extending knowledge beyond their known role in pollutant degradation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate combination of Hutchings' catalyst design for in situ H2O2 synthesis with visible-light photocatalysis on a tailored rutile platform for epoxidation, a reaction typically driven by thermal catalysis. The targeted use of chloride modification to tune both photocatalytic activity and oxidation selectivity in a single, integrated system is unexplored.

proposals.title: Deactivation and Regeneration of Pd-based Bimetallic Catalysts for In Situ H2O2 Synthesis in Methane Oxidation: The Role of Chloride-Induced Rutile Formation
proposals.motivation: The selective oxidation of methane to methanol using in situ generated H2O2 over Pd-based catalysts, pioneered by Hutchings' group, offers a promising route to valorize natural gas. However, catalyst longevity is a critical, unsolved challenge for industrial application. Recent work on chloride effects in photocatalytic titania systems suggests dissolved chlorides can profoundly influence oxide phase and stability, a factor likely relevant to the TiO2 (rutile) supports used in these methane oxidation catalysts but not yet investigated in this context.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chloride ions, present as impurities in feedstocks or from catalyst synthesis, accelerate the deactivation of Pd-Au/TiO2 (rutile) catalysts by promoting detrimental phase transformations or sintering of the rutile support and active metal nanoparticles, and that targeted regeneration protocols can restore catalytic activity by reversing these specific structural changes.
proposals.method: We will synthesize Pd-Au catalysts on rutile TiO2 and subject them to prolonged methane oxidation reaction conditions with controlled introduction of chloride salts (e.g., NaCl). Deactivation will be monitored via activity testing and characterized using in situ and ex situ techniques: XRD and Raman spectroscopy to track rutile-to-anatase phase changes and particle size; XPS and STEM-EDX to analyze chloride adsorption and metal nanoparticle dispersion. Regeneration protocols will be systematically tested, including oxidative treatments, acid washes, and thermal processing in chloride-free environments, with efficacy evaluated by restoring the original catalyst properties and activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct mechanistic link between chloride impurities and the structural deactivation of state-of-the-art methane-to-methanol catalysts. It will provide a validated, material-specific regeneration strategy, moving beyond trial-and-error. The findings will deliver critical design rules for catalyst stability against common poisons in real-world gas streams.
proposals.novelty: While chloride effects are known in photocatalysis, applying this understanding to the high-pressure, gas-phase thermocatalytic system for methane oxidation is novel. The study uniquely frames deactivation as an 'inverse problem' to catalyst design, using regeneration as a diagnostic tool to pinpoint the primary deactivation mechanism in a complex, multi-component catalytic system.

proposals.title: Tandem Catalysis on Rutile TiO2: In Situ H2O2 Synthesis for Methane Oxidation Followed by Selective Hydrogenation of the Resultant Dione
proposals.motivation: The selective oxidation of methane to methanol via in situ H2O2 generation, pioneered by Hutchings' group, is a critical route for methane valorization. However, over-oxidation to unwanted carbonyl compounds like glyoxal (ethanedione) remains a challenge. A complete catalytic system should not only produce oxygenates but also protect them, requiring a subsequent selective hydrogenation step to stabilize the desired products and improve overall yield.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the same rutile TiO2 supports, known to influence Pd-based catalyst properties for H2O2 synthesis and methane oxidation, can be co-functionalized with a secondary metal (e.g., Cu, Au) to selectively hydrogenate the dione byproduct (e.g., glyoxal) to a more stable diol (e.g., ethylene glycol) without degrading the initial methanol product.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of bimetallic catalysts (e.g., Pd-Cu, Pd-Au) supported on rutile TiO2, building directly on established pre-2026 methods. These will be tested in a two-stage reactor system: first, for in situ H2O2 synthesis and methane oxidation under reported conditions; second, the effluent will be directed over the same catalyst bed under adjusted temperature/H2 pressure for selective hydrogenation. Advanced characterization (XPS, HAADF-STEM, in-situ DRIFTS) will probe the distinct active sites and the influence of the rutile support's surface properties on both oxidative and reductive steps.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a novel tandem catalytic process on a single, multifunctional material, moving beyond single-step oxidation. It will provide fundamental insights into designing supports that stabilize metals for divergent reaction types (oxidation vs. hydrogenation). The outcome is a potential step-change in efficiency for catalytic methane-to-chemicals conversion by mitigating over-oxidation losses.
proposals.novelty: While rutile TiO2 is known for oxidation photocatalysis and H2O2 synthesis, its application as a stable support for a *combined* oxidative and reductive cascade in thermocatalysis, specifically to address the 'dual problem' within one catalytic cycle, is unexplored. The novelty lies in intentionally designing the catalyst to perform sequential, opposing reactions.

proposals.title: Combinatorial Synthesis and High-Throughput Screening of Au-Pd Bimetallic Catalysts for the Selective Oxidation of Methane via In Situ H2O2
proposals.motivation: The selective oxidation of methane to methanol using in situ generated H2O2 over Au-Pd catalysts, pioneered by Hutchings' group, is a promising route to valorize natural gas. However, the catalyst performance is highly sensitive to metal composition, particle size, and support identity, creating a vast multi-dimensional parameter space. Traditional one-at-a-time synthesis and testing is prohibitively slow for efficiently mapping optimal formulations, hindering the commercial development of this process.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that employing combinatorial inkjet printing for catalyst synthesis, coupled with high-throughput catalytic testing, will rapidly identify novel Au-Pd compositions and support materials (including chloride-modified titania) that significantly enhance methanol yield by optimizing the synergy between metal nanoparticles and support for H2O2 generation and methane activation.
proposals.method: We will develop a combinatorial synthesis platform using a piezoelectric inkjet printer to deposit precise micro-droplets of Au and Pd precursor solutions onto an array of different support wafers (e.g., TiO2 rutile/anatase, SiO2, carbon, and chloride-pre-treated variants). This will create a library of catalysts with controlled, graded variations in Au:Pd atomic ratio (e.g., 0:100 to 100:0) and support chemistry. The array will be processed (calcined, reduced) in a single batch. High-throughput screening will be performed using a parallel, miniaturized fixed-bed reactor system with mass spectrometry for product analysis, evaluating performance for the direct synthesis of H2O2 from H2 and O2 and its subsequent use for methane oxidation to methanol.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a rapid materials discovery pipeline for methane oxidation catalysts, directly yielding new, high-performance Au-Pd formulations. It will generate a comprehensive dataset correlating composition, support properties (including the influence of chlorides, linking to prior photocatalytic work), and activity/selectivity, providing fundamental insights into the active site requirements. The methodology will be a blueprint for accelerating catalyst development for other challenging selective oxidations.
proposals.novelty: While combinatorial methods exist, their application to the specific and complex challenge of tandem H2O2 synthesis and methane oxidation over Au-Pd is novel. Integrating support variation, including chloride-modified titania inspired by wastewater studies, with precise bimetallic composition control in a single high-throughput campaign represents a significant advance over prior sequential investigations.

proposals.title: In Situ Liquid Cell TEM Study of Palladium-Gold Cluster Dynamics During Methane Oxidation with In Situ H2O2 Generation
proposals.motivation: The selective oxidation of methane to methanol using in situ generated H2O2 is a critical challenge in catalysis. Pre-2026 work, including that from Hutchings' group, shows that bimetallic Pd-based catalysts are promising, but the dynamic structural evolution of active metal clusters under realistic liquid-phase reaction conditions, especially in the presence of halide ions known to influence activity, remains a 'black box'. Understanding these dynamics is key to rational catalyst design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the catalytic activity and selectivity of Pd-Au bimetallic clusters for methane oxidation are directly correlated with in situ observed structural dynamics—such as cluster coalescence, surface reconstruction, and leaching—which are critically modulated by the presence of chloride ions in the aqueous reaction medium.
proposals.method: We will employ in situ liquid cell transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with electrochemical control to observe model Pd-Au/TiO2 (rutile) catalysts. A custom liquid cell will be filled with an aqueous solution of H2, O2, and controlled concentrations of NaCl, mimicking the reaction environment for in situ H2O2 generation and methane oxidation. High-resolution TEM and scanning TEM (STEM) with EDS mapping will be used to track real-time changes in cluster size, composition, and morphology during simulated reaction conditions, correlating these dynamics with ex situ catalytic performance tests.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct visual evidence of the dynamic behavior of bimetallic clusters under working conditions for liquid-phase selective oxidation. It will establish a mechanistic link between chloride-induced effects (previously studied in photocatalysis) on catalyst stability and the methane oxidation pathway, moving beyond post-reaction characterization.
proposals.novelty: While liquid cell TEM has been used for nanoparticle growth studies, its application to observe the real-time dynamics of a working bimetallic catalyst during a complex, multi-step catalytic cycle (H2O2 synthesis followed by CH4 oxidation) in a tailored chemical environment is unprecedented.

proposals.title: Stability and Activity of Dynamic Palladium-Gold Clusters under High-Pressure Methane Oxidation Conditions
proposals.motivation: Recent pre-2026 work, including from Hutchings' group, has shown that dynamic, atomically dispersed or small clusters of Pd on Au or TiO2 (rutile) are highly active for the in situ synthesis of H2O2 and the selective oxidation of methane to methanol. However, these studies are typically conducted under mild, laboratory-scale conditions. For industrial translation, it is critical to understand whether these promising catalytic structures persist and remain selective under the high-pressure (tens of bar) and elevated temperature conditions required for viable process economics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dynamic, bimetallic Pd-Au clusters, which are active for H2O2 synthesis and methane activation under mild conditions, will undergo pressure- and temperature-dependent structural evolution, but that optimized support interactions (e.g., with rutile TiO2) can stabilize active configurations, maintaining high methanol selectivity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of Pd-Au catalysts supported on rutile TiO2, characterized pre-reaction by HAADF-STEM and XAS. Catalytic testing will be performed in a dedicated high-pressure continuous-flow reactor, varying pressure (5-50 bar) and temperature (50-150°C) while feeding CH4, O2, and H2 in a CO2-saturated aqueous medium. Post-reaction characterization (STEM, XAS) of quenched catalysts will be compared to pre-reaction states. The influence of chloride ions, known to affect TiO2 surface properties, will be studied as a potential stabilizing agent for metal clusters under harsh conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly bridge the gap between fundamental discovery of dynamic clusters and their practical application, providing a stability map for next-generation oxidation catalysts. It will establish structure-activity relationships under industrially relevant conditions and identify key factors (support, promoters, chlorides) that inhibit sintering or restructuring, guiding reactor design for methane valorization.
proposals.novelty: While the catalytic performance of Pd-Au clusters is known, their explicit structural fate under the combined high-pressure, high-temperature, and aqueous-phase conditions required for scale-up is unexplored. Linking this to the known influence of the TiO2 support phase and dissolved chlorides creates a unique, systems-level investigation.

proposals.title: Unambiguous Structure-Activity Mapping for Methane Oxidation: The Role of Defined Pd-Au Clusters in Direct H2O2 Synthesis and Utilization
proposals.motivation: The selective oxidation of methane to methanol using in situ generated H2O2 is a major challenge, with catalytic performance often attributed to ill-defined bilayer or nanoparticle phases. The influence of catalyst structure and the role of chlorides (from promoters or contaminants) on activity remain ambiguous, hindering rational design. This proposal addresses this by creating atomically precise, well-defined active sites to isolate their specific catalytic function.
proposals.hypothesis: That atomically precise, ligand-stabilized Pd-Au clusters of specific nuclearity (e.g., Pd4Au4), when grafted onto an inert rutile TiO2 support, will maintain their structural integrity under reaction conditions and provide a distinct, measurable catalytic signature for the direct synthesis and subsequent use of H2O2 in methane oxidation, free from interference from bilayer PdO_x phases.
proposals.method: Pre-formed, thiolate-capped Pd-Au clusters (e.g., Pd4Au4(SR)8) will be synthesized and grafted onto high-surface-area rutile TiO2. A two-stage characterization funnel will be employed: first, ex situ HAADF-STEM, XPS, and DRIFTS will confirm cluster deposition and the absence of PdO_x bilayers. Second, operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) and modulated excitation DRIFTS under methane, H2, and O2 flow will directly correlate the persistent electronic/geometric structure of the clusters with catalytic turnover, explicitly tracking the influence of chloride addition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive structure-activity relationship for non-bilayer, cluster-based active sites in direct H2O2 synthesis and methane oxidation. It will resolve attribution ambiguity in the literature and establish a rigorous methodology using operando spectroscopy on atomically precise precursors. The findings will guide the design of next-generation bimetallic catalysts for selective oxidation.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely combines pre-formed, molecularly defined bimetallic clusters with a rigorous operando characterization funnel to deconvolute the catalytic role of specific cluster geometries from traditional bilayer phases, a critical and unresolved issue in methane oxidation catalysis.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Optical Screening of Metal Clusters for Selective Oxidation of Organic Substrates Using Chromogenic Probes
proposals.motivation: The discovery of new catalysts for selective oxidation, a critical class of reactions for chemical synthesis, remains slow and labor-intensive. Hutchings' recent work on in situ H₂O₂ generation for methane oxidation and the influence of chlorides on titania properties highlights the need to rapidly map catalyst activity across diverse conditions and substrates. A platform enabling the parallel assessment of catalytic scope would dramatically accelerate the identification of promising materials for sustainable oxidation chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: That high-throughput screening (HTS) methodologies adapted from biochemical assays can be used to rapidly and quantitatively evaluate the activity and selectivity of supported metal cluster catalysts for a panel of liquid-phase oxidation reactions, revealing non-intuitive structure-activity relationships.
proposals.method: We will develop a 96-well plate screening platform using chromogenic or fluorogenic probe molecules for key oxidation reactions (e.g., alcohol to aldehyde, amine to imine). Candidate catalysts, including Pd-based bimetallic clusters and modified rutile TiO₂ systems informed by Hutchings' prior work, will be deposited in wells. Reactions will be initiated by adding substrate probes and a co-oxidant (e.g., O₂, or in situ generated H₂O₂). Catalytic activity will be quantified in parallel via plate-reader detection of optical changes (absorbance/fluorescence) associated with product formation, enabling rapid functional mapping.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel HTS workflow for heterogeneous catalysis discovery, moving beyond single-reaction testing. It will generate a unique dataset correlating cluster composition (including the influence of chlorides or promoters) with multi-substrate oxidation activity, identifying catalysts with broad scope or unexpected selectivity. This directly accelerates the development of efficient oxidation processes.
proposals.novelty: While HTS is standard in biochemistry, its application to screen solid heterogeneous catalysts for liquid-phase organic oxidation using optical chemical probes is highly innovative. This approach transcends traditional sequential testing, allowing for the direct, parallel observation of functional group tolerance and selectivity patterns unavailable through conventional methods.

proposals.title: Expanding the Catalytic Scope of Pd-Based Bimetallic Clusters: Selective Oxidation of C2-C4 Alkanes via In Situ H2O2 Synthesis
proposals.motivation: The selective partial oxidation of light alkanes (ethane, propane, butane) to oxygenates remains a significant industrial challenge, often requiring harsh conditions and suffering from over-oxidation. The recent Hutchings group breakthrough in methane-to-methanol conversion using in situ generated H2O2 over Pd-based bimetallic catalysts offers a promising, milder pathway. It is critical to determine if this catalytic system is uniquely tuned for methane or represents a general platform for alkane functionalization, which would greatly expand its impact.
proposals.hypothesis: The specific Pd-based bimetallic clusters (e.g., Pd-Au, Pd-Sn) active for the selective oxidation of methane via in situ H2O2 will demonstrate analogous activity and selectivity for the oxidation of higher homologous alkanes (C2-C4) to their corresponding alcohols and ketones, with catalytic performance being strongly influenced by the electronic properties imparted by the second metal and the reaction medium.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of supported Pd-M (M = Au, Sn, Fe, Cu) catalysts on TiO2 (rutile) and other carriers, characterized by HAADF-STEM, XPS, and CO-DRIFTS to identify cluster structures. Catalytic testing will involve batch reactor studies at 50-90°C and 10-30 bar, feeding alkane (C2H6, C3H8, n-C4H10), H2, and O2 in a continuous flow of water or a water/methanol solvent. Product analysis will use GC-MS and HPLC. We will systematically investigate the influence of dissolved chlorides (e.g., NaCl, HCl) on activity and selectivity, drawing on insights from photocatalytic degradation studies, to modulate reaction pathways and suppress over-oxidation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the broader applicability of the in situ H2O2 alkane oxidation strategy, potentially delivering novel catalytic routes to propylene glycol, acetone, and butanols. It will generate fundamental structure-activity relationships linking bimetallic cluster composition to performance across a homologous series, and elucidate the role of halide promoters in liquid-phase oxidation beyond methane.
proposals.novelty: While the system is proven for methane, its application to higher alkanes under these mild conditions is unexplored. The deliberate combination of bimetallic cluster catalysis with established halide promotion effects, informed by parallel wastewater treatment research, creates a unique, multi-variable approach to controlling selectivity in liquid-phase alkane oxidation.

proposals.title: Gram-Scale Synthesis of Au-Pd Bilayer Clusters on Rutile TiO2 for Enhanced In Situ H2O2 Generation and Methane Oxidation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Hutchings' own work, demonstrates that Au-Pd bilayer structures on titania are exceptionally active for the in situ synthesis of H2O2 and the subsequent selective oxidation of methane to methanol. However, these catalysts are typically synthesized in small batches (<100 mg) with inconsistent cluster structures, hindering reliable performance evaluation and practical application. Developing a scalable, reproducible synthesis method is a critical bottleneck for advancing this promising catalytic route.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by controlling the sequential deposition kinetics of Au and Pd precursors onto a rutile TiO2 support in a continuous flow reactor, we can achieve gram-scale production of catalysts where the dominant active site is a Pd-on-Au bilayer cluster, leading to superior and consistent activity for methane oxidation via in situ H2O2 synthesis.
proposals.method: We will employ a scalable, continuous impregnation method using a microfluidic reactor to ensure uniform precursor mixing and deposition. Rutile TiO2 powder will be suspended and circulated, with precise control over temperature, pH, and chloride concentration (informed by Hutchings' work on chloride effects) during the sequential addition of HAuCl4 and Na2PdCl4. Post-synthesis, catalysts will be characterized using HAADF-STEM, XPS, and CO-DRIFTS to quantify bilayer cluster prevalence. Catalytic testing will involve measuring H2O2 synthesis rates from H2/O2 and subsequent methane oxidation activity in a fixed-bed reactor.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust, scalable synthesis protocol for a highly active bilayer catalyst, moving beyond lab-scale demonstrations. It will establish clear structure-activity relationships by providing sufficient material for statistically rigorous testing and will enable further studies on catalyst stability and reactor design for direct methane valorization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in translating the synthesis of a structurally precise, bimetallic cluster—previously a nanoscale laboratory curiosity—into a scalable, continuous manufacturing process, explicitly targeting gram quantities of a catalyst dominated by a single, well-defined active site morphology.

proposals.title: Defining the Nuclearity of the Active Site in Pd-based Catalysts for the Selective Oxidation of Methane via In Situ H₂O₂
proposals.motivation: The selective oxidation of methane to methanol using in situ generated H₂O₂ over Pd-based catalysts, pioneered by Hutchings' group, is a transformative route for methane valorization. However, the precise nuclearity of the active Pd species—whether mononuclear, dinuclear, or clustered—remains ambiguous, hindering rational catalyst design. This mechanistic uncertainty is exacerbated by the potential influence of chloride ions, known from related work to alter titania surface properties, which may also affect Pd speciation and stability under reaction conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: The active site for methane oxidation is a specific, discrete Pd nuclearity (e.g., Pd dimer or defined cluster), whose formation and stability are controlled by the reaction environment, including the presence of chloride ions. This defined species, not a distribution of sites, is responsible for the observed catalytic activity and selectivity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of well-defined Pd complexes (monomeric, dimeric, and small clusters) supported on rutile TiO₂ as spectroscopic and kinetic benchmarks. Using operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) and diffuse reflectance UV-Vis, we will perform hypothesis-neutral speciation analysis on working catalysts. Kinetic studies, including order-in-Pd experiments and selective poisoning with sulfur-containing compounds, will be correlated with the spectroscopic data to link specific Pd nuclearities to turnover. The influence of chloride, a known modifier of metal oxide surfaces, will be systematically varied to probe its role in stabilizing active polynuclear sites.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a definitive, experimentally-verified model for the active site nuclearity in a critically important methane oxidation system. It will establish a rigorous methodology combining defined synthesis, operando spectroscopy, and kinetics to resolve active site debates in heterogeneous catalysis. The findings will provide a clear design principle for next-generation bimetallic catalysts based on controlled nuclearity.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its hypothesis-neutral, multi-technique correlation to directly assign function to specific metal nuclearities, moving beyond indirect inference. It uniquely integrates the synthesis of discrete model complexes on relevant supports (rutile TiO₂) with operando analysis under realistic catalytic conditions, including the deliberate use of chloride as a speciation probe.

proposals.title: Engineering Pre-Catalytic Ensembles: The Role of Oxide Support Properties in Directing Metal Precursor Aggregation During Deposition
proposals.motivation: The genesis of active sites in supported metal catalysts is critically influenced by the initial state of the metal precursor during deposition. Current models often treat metal aggregation as a property of the solution-phase chemistry, neglecting the active role of the support. Understanding how support properties dictate precursor aggregation is essential for the rational design of catalysts, particularly for demanding selective oxidation reactions where ensemble size controls selectivity, as seen in Hutchings' work on methane-to-methanol via in situ H₂O₂.
proposals.hypothesis: The aggregation state of a metal precursor (e.g., Pd, Au) during wet impregnation is not solely solution-determined but is actively templated by specific physicochemical properties of the oxide support, such as its point of zero charge, surface hydroxyl density, and redox activity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of Pd-based catalysts via incipient wetness impregnation using identical precursor solutions (e.g., PdCl₂) onto model supports: rutile TiO₂, anatase TiO₂, CeO₂, and SiO₂. Advanced characterization, including aberration-corrected STEM-HAADF and in situ DRIFTS, will quantify the aggregation state (isolated ions, oligomers, nanoparticles) of the metal after deposition and low-temperature drying. These states will be correlated with support properties measured by ζ-potentiometry (PZC), TPD-MS (hydroxyl density), and XPS (redox state). Catalytic testing for methane oxidation using in situ generated H₂O₂ will link initial aggregation to activity and selectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a predictive framework linking fundamental support properties to the initial metal speciation, providing a new design rule for catalyst synthesis. It will directly inform the optimization of bimetallic catalysts for selective oxidation by enabling control over pre-catalytic ensembles. The findings will challenge the field to view the support as an active director of synthesis, not just a passive carrier.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the conventional paradigm by proposing that the solid support actively templates solution-derived aggregation, rather than merely accepting pre-formed aggregates. It systematically decouples solution and support effects, a crucial but overlooked distinction in catalyst synthesis.

proposals.title: Probing the Locus of Hydrogen Peroxide Decomposition: A Kinetic and Spectroscopic Study of Metal-Support Interactions in Pd-based Catalysts for Methane Oxidation
proposals.motivation: The selective oxidation of methane to methanol using in situ generated H2O2 is a promising route, but efficiency is often limited by the non-productive decomposition of H2O2. The prevailing assumption in many studies is that this decomposition occurs primarily on acidic or defect sites of the catalyst support (e.g., TiO2). However, this oversimplification may obscure the role of the active metal nanoparticles themselves, hindering the rational design of more stable and selective catalysts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that under reaction conditions for methane oxidation, Pd and Pd-based bimetallic nanoparticles (e.g., Pd-Au, Pd-Sn) are significant, active sites for H2O2 decomposition, and that this activity is modulated by the electronic properties of the metal induced by the support and by the presence of dissolved chlorides.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of Pd and Pd-bimetallic catalysts on well-defined rutile TiO2 supports with controlled surface properties. Kinetic analysis of H2O2 decomposition rates will be performed separately from methane oxidation, using isotopic labelling (D2O2) and selective poisoning experiments with cyanide ions to distinguish metal vs. support sites. In situ Raman and UV-Vis spectroscopy will monitor the formation and decay of surface peroxo species. The influence of chloride ions, relevant to wastewater conditions, will be systematically studied to assess their impact on both metal and support-mediated decomposition pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct, quantitative evidence partitioning H2O2 decomposition activity between metal and support sites, establishing a more complete mechanistic picture. It will deliver design principles for mitigating H2O2 loss by engineering metal-support interfaces and selecting appropriate promoters or inhibitors, thereby advancing catalyst development for selective oxidation processes.
proposals.novelty: While the influence of supports on metal activity is known, explicitly challenging the dogma that H2O2 decomposition is solely a support-driven phenomenon in these systems is novel. Integrating the study of chloride effects, from Hutchings' prior work on photocatalysis, into this catalytic oxidation context creates a unique cross-disciplinary insight.

proposals.title: Optimizing Palladium-Tin Catalysts for Methane Oxidation via In Situ H2O2: A Trade-off Between Methanol Yield and Hydrogen Efficiency
proposals.motivation: The direct, low-temperature oxidation of methane to methanol using in situ generated H2O2 is a major pre-2026 challenge. While high hydrogen selectivity (H2 used for H2O2 synthesis) is often targeted to minimize feedstock cost, this can severely limit the overall rate of methanol production. A holistic optimization focusing on process economics, rather than a single metric, is needed to advance towards practical application.
proposals.hypothesis: By systematically relaxing the constraint of high hydrogen selectivity in Pd-based bimetallic catalysts, we can identify compositions and conditions that maximize the net methanol production rate and overall process efficiency, even at the expense of some H2 conversion to H2O2.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of Pd-M (M = Sn, Cu, Au) catalysts supported on TiO2 (rutile), informed by Hutchings' prior work. Catalytic testing will involve methane oxidation in a H2/O2 mixture, with rigorous product analysis (CH3OH, H2O2, CO2). We will deliberately vary parameters known to influence hydrogen selectivity (e.g., Pd:M ratio, chloride concentration in the synthesis medium, as per Hutchings' chloride-effect studies) to create a performance matrix. The primary optimization function will be a cost-adjusted yield metric incorporating methanol production rate and H2 consumption.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive map of the trade-off between hydrogen efficiency and methanol yield for in situ H2O2 systems. It will identify catalyst formulations and operating windows optimized for overall process performance, moving the field beyond a singular focus on selectivity. The study will also elucidate how chloride ions and promoter metals influence this fundamental trade-off.
proposals.novelty: Shifting the optimization target from maximal hydrogen selectivity to overall process rate/cost represents a paradigm shift for this reaction. It systematically leverages known catalyst properties (rutile support, bimetallic promotion, chloride influence) to solve a different, more practical engineering-economic problem.

proposals.title: Decoupling H2O2 Synthesis and Deactivation Pathways in Methane Oxidation via Single-Atom and Non-Alloyed Catalysts
proposals.motivation: The direct synthesis of H2O2 for in situ methane oxidation is a promising route to methanol, but state-of-the-art catalysts (e.g., Pd-Au alloys) inherently couple the sites for H2O2 generation and decomposition, limiting yield. Furthermore, Hutchings' recent work on chloride effects in photocatalytic titania systems suggests that specific adsorbed anions can profoundly influence surface reactivity, a principle underexplored in this context. Decoupling these functions is a critical, unresolved challenge for improving selectivity and activity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by using precisely engineered single-atom catalysts (SACs) or spatially separated non-alloyed nanoparticles, we can isolate the function of H2O2 synthesis from its decomposition, while strategically introduced chloride ligands can modulate the oxidation activity of the methane activation sites.
proposals.method: We will synthesize Pd single-atom catalysts on tailored rutile TiO2 supports, alongside physical mixtures of discrete Pd and Pt nanoparticles on the same support to create non-alloyed systems. These will be tested for the direct synthesis of H2O2 from H2 and O2 and its subsequent use in methane oxidation. Advanced characterization (AC-STEM, XAS) will confirm site isolation. Building on Hutchings' chloride studies, we will systematically introduce controlled amounts of chloride anions (e.g., as HCl or NaCl) into the aqueous reaction medium to probe their influence on catalyst stability, H2O2 selectivity, and methane activation pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a fundamental understanding of how catalyst architecture (alloyed vs. isolated sites) controls the competing pathways of H2O2 synthesis and decomposition. It will establish design principles for next-generation catalysts that maximize the effective concentration of H2O2 for selective oxidation. The study will also elucidate the role of halide modifiers, moving beyond their known poisoning effects to potential beneficial roles in tuning selectivity.
proposals.novelty: The proposal moves decisively beyond the prevailing alloy nanoparticle paradigm for direct H2O2 synthesis, explicitly testing the decoupling hypothesis using SACs and non-alloyed mixtures. It uniquely combines this architectural approach with mechanistic insights from Hutchings' chloride-effect studies, applying them to a high-impact catalytic oxidation challenge.

proposals.title: Acid Pretreatment as a General Strategy to Enhance Direct H2O2 Synthesis: Extending the Concept from Pd to Au-Pd, Pt-Pd, and Monometallic Catalysts
proposals.motivation: Direct synthesis of H2O2 from H2 and O2 offers a green, decentralized alternative to the energy-intensive anthraquinone process, but its efficiency is limited by low selectivity and H2O2 decomposition. Pre-2026 work by Hutchings and others demonstrated that acid pretreatment of Pd-based catalysts (e.g., with HNO3) can leach surface Pd, creating favorable sites and improving performance. However, this strategy remains largely unexplored for other promising catalytic systems, limiting the development of optimized, cost-effective catalysts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that controlled acid pretreatment is a generalizable strategy that can selectively modify the surface structure and electronic properties of diverse bimetallic (Au-Pd, Pt-Pd) and monometallic (Pd, Au) catalysts, thereby enhancing their activity and selectivity for direct H2O2 synthesis by suppressing undesirable side reactions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of supported catalysts (Pd/TiO2, Au-Pd/TiO2, Pt-Pd/TiO2) using established deposition-precipitation and impregnation methods. These catalysts will be subjected to systematic pretreatment with dilute mineral acids (HNO3, HCl) at varying concentrations, temperatures, and durations. The influence of pretreatment will be rigorously characterized using pre-2026 techniques: N2 physisorption for surface area, XRD for bulk structure, XPS for surface composition/oxidation state, and STEM-EDX for elemental distribution. Catalytic testing for direct H2O2 synthesis will be performed in a slurry reactor under standard conditions (2-4 bar, 2°C in methanol/water), with H2O2 yield and selectivity determined by ceric sulfate titration.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the scope and mechanistic principles of acid pretreatment as a versatile catalyst engineering tool for H2O2 synthesis. It will identify new, high-performance catalyst formulations and provide fundamental insights into the role of surface composition and acidity in controlling the delicate balance between H2O2 formation and decomposition/hydrogenation pathways.
proposals.novelty: While acid pretreatment is known for Pd catalysts, its targeted application to modulate the surface structure of Au-Pd and Pt-Pd bimetallics for this reaction is novel. Extending the concept to monometallic Au catalysts, where surface restructuring could uniquely activate O2, represents a significant and non-obvious departure from existing Pd-centric studies.

proposals.title: Deactivation Mechanisms and Regenerative Strategies for Acid-Pretreated Pd-Au/TiO2 Catalysts in the Selective Oxidation of Methane via In Situ H2O2
proposals.motivation: The selective oxidation of methane to methanol using in situ generated H2O2 over Pd-based catalysts is a promising route for valorizing natural gas. However, long-term stability is a critical barrier to commercialization. Recent work by Hutchings and others shows acid pretreatment of TiO2 (rutile) supports enhances initial activity, but the durability of this enhancement and the influence of reaction by-products, like chlorides from synthesis or feed, on deactivation are unknown.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the primary deactivation mechanisms for acid-pretreated Pd-Au/TiO2 catalysts are sintering of Pd-Au nanoparticles and chloride poisoning of active sites, and that targeted oxidative thermal treatments can selectively remove chlorides and re-disperse metals to restore catalytic activity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and acid-pretreat rutile TiO2 supports, then deposit Pd-Au nanoparticles. Catalysts will be subjected to extended time-on-stream testing (100+ hours) for methane oxidation with in situ H2O2 generation. Post-reaction characterization (TEM, XPS, XRD) will identify deactivation modes (sintering, coking, chloride adsorption). Regeneration protocols, including controlled temperature-programmed oxidation in air and mild acid washing, will be systematically applied. The influence of dissolved chlorides in the aqueous reaction medium on deactivation rates will be specifically studied, linking to Hutchings' prior work on chloride effects in photocatalysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a fundamental understanding of deactivation pathways in a promising methane-to-methanol catalyst system. It will deliver practical, optimized regeneration protocols to extend catalyst lifetime, a key economic parameter. The study will also clarify the role of chlorides, a common impurity, in both promoting (via support modification) and poisoning catalytic function.
proposals.novelty: While catalyst deactivation is widely studied, a systematic investigation linking the beneficial effects of acid pretreatment (creating specific surface properties) to subsequent vulnerability to specific poisons like chloride, and developing tailored regeneration methods for these advanced bimetallic systems, is novel and non-obvious.

proposals.title: Scalable Continuous-Flow Synthesis of Acid-Pretreated Au-Pd/C Catalysts for Enhanced In Situ H2O2 Generation and Methane Oxidation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work by Hutchings and others has demonstrated that acid pretreatment of carbon-supported Au-Pd catalysts significantly enhances their activity for the in situ synthesis of H2O2, a key oxidant for the selective oxidation of methane to methanol. However, the reported synthesis methods are limited to small, labor-intensive batch processes, creating a major bottleneck for industrial evaluation and deployment. Developing a scalable, reproducible manufacturing route is therefore critical to translating this promising catalytic chemistry from the laboratory to practical application.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a continuous-flow impregnation and acid-washing process can be engineered to produce Au-Pd/C catalysts with consistent and enhanced catalytic properties (activity, selectivity, stability) comparable to or exceeding those made by optimized batch methods, by precisely controlling metal deposition and chloride removal.
proposals.method: We will design and construct a continuous flow reactor system for catalyst synthesis. This will involve the sequential, controlled pumping of a carbon slurry (e.g., activated carbon) and metal precursor solutions (HAuCl4, PdCl2) into a static mixer for impregnation, followed by a heated tubular section for aging/deposition. The resultant solid will be continuously filtered and transferred to a second flow system for acid washing (using HNO3 at controlled concentration, temperature, and residence time) to remove chloride ions and modify the metal nanoparticle surface. Key process variables (flow rates, concentrations, temperatures, wash duration) will be systematically optimized using a design-of-experiments approach, with catalyst performance evaluated in a standardized test reaction (direct synthesis of H2O2 from H2 and O2).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first reported methodology for the continuous, scalable production of acid-pretreated bimetallic catalysts, directly addressing a materials supply challenge in oxidation catalysis. It will establish the critical process parameters linking synthesis conditions to catalytic performance for H2O2 generation, offering a blueprint for scaling other pretreated catalyst systems. The resulting catalysts will enable more robust testing of methane oxidation processes at relevant scales.
proposals.novelty: While the beneficial effect of acid pretreatment is known, the engineering of a continuous manufacturing process for these specific catalysts is unexplored. The novelty lies in integrating known chemical steps (impregnation, chloride removal) into a controlled flow process to achieve consistent, high-performance catalyst production at scale, moving beyond empirical batch synthesis.

proposals.title: A Microstructured Reactor for the Direct Synthesis of H2O2 from H2/O2 Mixtures: Enhancing Safety and Selectivity via Pd-based Bimetallic Catalysts
proposals.motivation: The direct synthesis of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) from H2 and O2 offers a green, decentralized alternative to the energy-intensive anthraquinone process. However, the use of explosive H2/O2 mixtures in conventional reactors presents significant safety hazards and often leads to low selectivity due to consecutive H2O2 hydrogenation and decomposition. Pre-2026, Hutchings' group has pioneered Pd-based bimetallic catalysts for in situ H2O2 generation in methane oxidation, but their implementation in inherently safer reactor designs is underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a tailored Pd-Au (or Pd-Sn) bimetallic catalyst, optimized for H2O2 synthesis, into a continuous-flow microstructured reactor will significantly enhance process safety by minimizing explosive gas volumes and improve H2O2 selectivity through precise control of residence time and mass transfer.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a stainless-steel microreactor with integrated cooling channels and a structured catalyst bed. A Pd-based bimetallic catalyst (e.g., Pd-Au/TiO2-rutile), selected based on Hutchings' prior work on methane oxidation, will be wash-coated onto the microchannels. The influence of reactor geometry, flow regime, pressure (10-30 bar), temperature (2-30 °C), and gas composition (H2/O2 ratio, with inert diluent) on H2O2 productivity and selectivity will be systematically evaluated. Online analytics (UV-Vis for H2O2 titration, GC for gas analysis) will enable real-time monitoring.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated prototype for the safe, intensified direct synthesis of H2O2, bridging the gap between catalyst design and reactor engineering. It will deliver fundamental insights into how microreactor environments influence the catalytic pathways for H2O2 formation versus degradation, contributing to the broader field of selective oxidation.
proposals.novelty: While microreactors for H2O2 synthesis exist, the novelty lies in the specific integration of Hutchings' class of bimetallic catalysts—previously studied for in situ use in methane oxidation—into a dedicated microreactor platform designed to exploit their properties for the standalone H2O2 synthesis reaction with enhanced safety control.

proposals.title: Integrated On-Site H2O2 Generation and Utilisation for the Selective Oxidation of Methane to Methanol over Pd-Au/TiO2 Catalysts
proposals.motivation: The direct synthesis of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) offers a safer, decentralized alternative to the energy-intensive anthraquinone process, but its application is hindered by H2O2's instability during storage and transport. Concurrently, the selective oxidation of methane to methanol using in situ generated H2O2 is a promising route to valorize natural gas, but efficiency is limited by the low concentration and rapid decomposition of H2O2 in batch systems. Integrating these two processes in a single, continuous flow system would circumvent these critical stability and concentration challenges.
proposals.hypothesis: That coupling a direct H2O2 synthesis reactor with a methane oxidation reactor in a single, integrated flow system will significantly enhance the overall methanol yield by providing a continuous, fresh supply of H2O2 at optimal concentration, thereby minimizing its decomposition and maximizing its utilization for selective methane oxidation.
proposals.method: We will design and construct a dual-bed, continuous flow reactor system. The first bed will contain a Pd-Au bimetallic catalyst supported on TiO2 (rutile) for the direct synthesis of H2O2 from H2 and O2 in a methanol/water solvent. The effluent from this bed, containing dissolved H2O2, will be immediately fed, without intermediate storage, into a second catalytic bed containing a tailored Pd-based catalyst (e.g., Pd-Cu) for the selective oxidation of methane. We will systematically study the influence of reactor configuration (co-current vs. staged), space velocity, pressure, and the effect of chloride ions (introduced as HCl or NaCl) on both the H2O2 synthesis rate and the subsequent methane oxidation activity and selectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a foundational proof-of-concept for process-intensified, on-demand oxidative catalysis, moving beyond batch studies of in situ H2O2 use. It will deliver new insights into the kinetic coupling of two catalytic cycles in a flow system and establish design principles for integrated reactors that avoid hazardous intermediate handling. The study of chloride influence will bridge Hutchings' recent work on wastewater photocatalysis with his methane oxidation research.
proposals.novelty: While direct H2O2 synthesis and its use for methane oxidation have been studied separately, their real-time integration into a single, uninterrupted flow process is novel. This approach fundamentally shifts the paradigm from 'synthesize-then-use' to 'synthesize-and-immediately-use,' a non-obvious engineering solution to the inherent instability of H2O2 in catalytic cycles.

proposals.title: Fe-Cu Synergy on Metal-Organic Framework Supports for the Selective Oxidation of Methane via In Situ H2O2
proposals.motivation: The Fe-Cu synergy in zeolites, such as Fe-MFI and Cu-MFI, is a preeminent pre-2026 strategy for the direct, low-temperature oxidation of methane to methanol using in situ generated H2O2. However, the microporous, aluminosilicate nature of zeolites imposes diffusion limitations and restricts active site engineering. Exploring this powerful bimetallic motif on alternative, tunable supports could unlock superior activity and selectivity by overcoming these inherent zeolitic constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the cooperative redox chemistry between Fe and Cu species, which facilitates H2O2 activation and C-H bond cleavage, can be effectively transposed and enhanced when anchored on highly porous, functionalizable Metal-Organic Framework (MOF) supports, leading to improved methane-to-methanol conversion rates.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of Fe-Cu bimetallic catalysts via post-synthetic modification of robust, mesoporous MOFs (e.g., MIL-101(Cr) or NU-1000), chosen for their high surface area, hydrothermal stability, and well-defined coordination sites. Catalytic testing will follow Hutchings-group protocols: methane oxidation in a H2/O2 mixture at low temperature (<100°C) in a batch reactor, with product analysis by GC. The influence of MOF topology, linker functionality, and Fe/Cu ratio on activity will be systematically evaluated. Advanced characterization (XAS, EPR, DRIFTS) will probe the nature and proximity of the Fe-Cu sites on the MOF scaffold.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new materials platform for Fe-Cu methane oxidation catalysts, moving beyond traditional zeolites. It will provide fundamental insights into how support porosity and chemical environment modulate this critical bimetallic synergy. The findings could directly inform the design of next-generation catalysts for natural gas valorization.
proposals.novelty: While Fe-Cu-zeolite systems are well-studied, their deliberate immobilization on MOF supports to exploit superior porosity and site-isolation is unexplored. This proposal uniquely merges Hutchings' expertise in catalytic methane oxidation with the emerging field of MOF-based catalysis.

proposals.title: Non-Aqueous Selective Oxidation of Methane via In Situ Peroxide Generation over Pd-Based Catalysts: The Role of Solvent and Chloride Speciation
proposals.motivation: The selective oxidation of methane to methanol using in situ generated H2O2 in aqueous media, pioneered by Hutchings' group, faces challenges including over-oxidation and limited methane solubility. Pre-2026 work shows dissolved chlorides significantly influence titania photocatalyst properties, suggesting solvent environment critically controls reaction pathways. Exploring non-aqueous systems could unlock new regimes of activity and selectivity by fundamentally altering peroxide generation, stabilization, and reactant interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that employing non-aqueous solvents (e.g., methanol, acetonitrile) or hydrophobic ionic liquids will enhance methane solubility and modify the speciation/reactivity of in situ generated peroxides and any chloride promoters, leading to improved methanol selectivity and yield by suppressing undesirable over-oxidation pathways.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of Pd-Au bimetallic catalysts supported on rutile TiO2, a phase known for its stability. Catalytic testing will be performed in batch reactors using varied solvent systems: water (baseline), organic solvents (methanol, acetonitrile), and hydrophobic ionic liquids (e.g., [BMIM][PF6]). Reactions will be conducted under controlled CH4/O2/H2 feeds. Advanced characterization (XPS, in-situ DRIFTS) will probe catalyst surface state and chloride distribution. The influence of solvent on H2O2 formation rate, stability, and methane oxidation pathways will be decoupled through kinetic isotope experiments and controlled addition of H2O2.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fundamental understanding of how solvent polarity and hydrophobicity govern the efficiency of in situ peroxide generation and its subsequent use in selective oxidation. It will provide a new strategy to circumvent aqueous-phase limitations in methane valorization and elucidate the role of chlorides beyond aqueous electrolytes, impacting catalyst design for other selective oxidations.
proposals.novelty: While the aqueous-phase process is established, systematically challenging the necessity of water as the solvent for this reaction is novel. Combining Hutchings' expertise in methane oxidation and chloride effects with non-aqueous reaction engineering creates a distinct approach to controlling selectivity.

proposals.title: Assessing the Long-Term Stability of Pd-Based Bimetallic Catalysts for the Selective Oxidation of Methane via In Situ H2O2 Synthesis
proposals.motivation: The selective oxidation of methane to methanol using in situ generated H2O2 represents a promising route for valorizing natural gas. However, the practical deployment of such catalytic processes requires catalysts that are not only initially active and selective but also stable over extended periods and multiple reaction cycles. Pre-2026 work by Hutchings and others has demonstrated promising initial performance, but comprehensive long-term stability data under realistic, potentially chloride-containing conditions is lacking, hindering techno-economic assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the structural and compositional integrity of Pd-based bimetallic catalysts (e.g., Pd-Au, Pd-Pt) will degrade over extended cycling, with the rate and nature of deactivation being critically influenced by the presence of dissolved chlorides, which are known to affect titania properties and may leach metals or modify active sites.
proposals.method: We will perform extended reusability tests (target: >50 cycles) on established Pd-based bimetallic catalysts supported on rutile TiO2, using the selective methane oxidation reaction with in situ H2O2 synthesis. Each cycle will consist of a standard batch reaction followed by catalyst recovery. We will systematically vary the concentration of dissolved chlorides (e.g., NaCl) in the aqueous reaction medium to mimic industrial water sources. Catalyst characterization (XRD, XPS, TEM, ICP-MS) will be conducted at regular intervals to correlate changes in activity/selectivity with physical and chemical properties, such as metal leaching, particle sintering, and support surface modification.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed, long-cycle stability map for this important class of methane oxidation catalysts, explicitly linking deactivation mechanisms to chloride ion concentration. It will deliver critical data for reactor design and catalyst regeneration strategies, moving the field from proof-of-concept demonstrations towards evaluating practical viability.
proposals.novelty: While catalyst reusability is often tested for 3-5 cycles, the systematic investigation over 50+ cycles combined with the deliberate study of chloride-induced deactivation—inspired by Hutchings' parallel work on chlorides in photocatalysis—is novel. This approach integrates insights from different catalytic domains (thermal and photo-) to probe a key, underexplored practical constraint.

proposals.title: Operando XAS Elucidation of Pd Oxidation State Dynamics During In Situ H2O2 Synthesis for Methane Oxidation in Chloride-Containing Media
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, Hutchings' work demonstrates that in situ H2O2 synthesis over Pd-based catalysts enables the selective oxidation of methane to methanol, a critical reaction for utilizing natural gas. However, the precise oxidation state of the active Pd species under reaction conditions, especially in aqueous environments containing dissolved chlorides (known to influence titania photocatalysis), remains ambiguous. Understanding these dynamic redox states is crucial for rational catalyst design, as chloride ions can dramatically alter metal speciation and stability.
