proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the presence of chloride ions in the aqueous reaction medium dynamically modulates the oxidation state of palladium in bimetallic catalysts (e.g., Pd-Au, Pd-Sn) during the catalytic cycle of in situ H2O2 generation and subsequent methane oxidation, and that maintaining a specific, mixed Pd(0)/Pd(II) state is key to high methanol selectivity.
proposals.method: We will employ operando X-ray Absorption Spectroscopy (XAS), specifically XANES and EXAFS, at a synchrotron beamline (e.g., Diamond Light Source I20) to monitor Pd K-edge spectra. A custom-designed, corrosion-resistant catalytic reactor cell will enable real-time analysis of supported Pd-based bimetallic catalysts during methane oxidation in water with controlled chloride concentrations (0-1000 ppm). Spectra will be collected under steady-state reaction conditions (e.g., 50°C, 30 bar, H2/O2/CH4 feed) and correlated with simultaneous online product analysis via mass spectrometry and chromatography to link oxidation state to activity/selectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, time-resolved spectroscopic evidence of the active Pd species in Hutchings' promising methane-to-methanol process, establishing a structure-activity relationship under genuine reaction conditions. It will quantify the influence of chloride, a common impurity in water and industrial streams, on catalyst stability and redox dynamics, offering critical insights for designing robust, selective oxidation catalysts.
proposals.novelty: While operando XAS is established in fuel cell electrochemistry, its application to monitor metal states in this specific aqueous-phase thermocatalytic system, particularly with a focus on halide ion effects informed by parallel photocatalytic studies, is novel. The direct transfer of this advanced diagnostic technique to a pressing catalytic challenge represents a significant methodological crossover.

proposals.title: Gas-Phase Selective Oxidation of Methane to Methanol via In Situ H2O2 Generation Over Rutile-Supported Bimetallic Catalysts
proposals.motivation: The aqueous-phase selective oxidation of methane using in situ generated H2O2, pioneered by Hutchings' group, faces challenges including methanol over-oxidation and catalyst leaching due to the aqueous solvent. A gas-phase system could circumvent these issues, potentially improving selectivity and catalyst stability. This addresses a core bottleneck in the direct catalytic conversion of methane, a critical pathway for utilizing natural gas.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that performing the methane oxidation reaction in a dry gas phase, using a tailored rutile-supported Pd-based bimetallic catalyst, will suppress unwanted sequential oxidation pathways and enhance methanol selectivity by avoiding the solvent effects of liquid water.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of Pd-Au and Pd-Cu bimetallic nanoparticles supported on rutile TiO2, leveraging known synergistic effects for H2O2 formation. Catalytic testing will be performed in a fixed-bed flow reactor under controlled gas feeds (CH4, O2, H2) at moderate temperatures (50-150°C) and pressures (10-30 bar). The influence of trace chlorides, known to modify titania surface properties, will be investigated as a promoter by pre-treating the rutile support. Reaction products will be analyzed via online GC-MS and FTIR.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for a gas-phase methane-to-methanol process using in situ H2O2, directly comparing its performance metrics to established aqueous systems. It will provide fundamental insights into the role of the reaction medium on intermediate stability and catalyst function, guiding the design of next-generation oxidation catalysts.
proposals.novelty: While Hutchings' work focuses on aqueous-phase systems, the deliberate exclusion of bulk water as a solvent for this specific reaction pathway is unexplored. The combination of a gas-phase approach with the use of chloride-modified rutile supports, informed by photocatalytic degradation studies, creates a unique strategy to control surface reactivity.

proposals.title: Elucidating the Role of Single-Metal Sites in the Radical Pathway for Methane Oxidation via In Situ H₂O₂ Synthesis
proposals.motivation: The selective oxidation of methane to methanol using in situ generated H₂O₂ represents a promising route to valorize natural gas, but current bimetallic catalysts (e.g., Pd-Au) introduce complexity and cost. Prior work by Hutchings and others has focused on synergistic effects in bimetallics, leaving the fundamental activity of individual metal components for this specific radical-initiating pathway unclear. Understanding if a single metal can efficiently catalyze both H₂O₂ formation and subsequent radical generation is crucial for simplifying catalyst design and improving economic viability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that single metals, particularly Au or Pd, supported on appropriate carriers, can effectively catalyze the in situ H₂O₂ synthesis and Fenton-like activation required for the radical-based oxidation of methane to methanol, without the need for a second metal partner.
proposals.method: We will prepare a series of single-metal (Au, Pd, Pt, Fe) catalysts supported on rutile TiO₂, chosen for its established role in related photocatalytic and radical processes. Catalysts will be tested in a fixed-bed reactor under conditions (temperature, pressure, CH₄/H₂/O₂ ratios) mirroring prior bimetallic studies. Activity and selectivity will be correlated with detailed characterization (XRD, XPS, HAADF-STEM) to link metal oxidation state, particle size, and dispersion to performance. Crucially, we will employ radical trap experiments (e.g., using DMPO) and isotopic labeling to confirm the operation and origin of the radical pathway.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the necessity of bimetallic synergy for this reaction, potentially identifying simpler, high-performance single-metal catalysts. It will provide fundamental insights into the active sites responsible for H₂O₂ generation and activation on isolated metals, advancing the mechanistic understanding of radical-mediated methane oxidation.
proposals.novelty: While bimetallic systems are the state-of-the-art, systematically investigating single metals for this specific coupled H₂O₂ synthesis/radical oxidation pathway is novel. The approach deliberately decouples the roles often ascribed to individual metals in alloys, probing if a single site can fulfill both functions.

proposals.title: Selective Oxidation of Ethane to Oxygenates via In Situ Generated H2O2 and Methyl-like Radicals over Rutile-Supported Bimetallic Catalysts
proposals.motivation: The selective partial oxidation of light alkanes beyond methane, such as ethane, remains a significant chemical challenge with major industrial implications for producing valuable oxygenated feedstocks like ethanol and acetaldehyde. Pre-2026 work by Hutchings and others has established a promising pathway for methane using in situ H2O2 generation to form methyl radicals, but the extension to higher alkanes is underexplored. Understanding how catalyst properties and reaction environments influence the radical-mediated oxidation of ethane could unlock more efficient routes for natural gas liquid valorization.
proposals.hypothesis: The radical-mediated mechanism, proven for methane-to-methanol conversion via in situ H2O2, can be extended to selectively oxidize ethane to C2 oxygenates (ethanol/acetaldehyde) by generating ethyl radicals under controlled conditions. We hypothesize that the surface properties of rutile-supported Pd-based bimetallic catalysts and the presence of chloride ions will critically influence the selectivity by modulating radical formation and subsequent O2 incorporation pathways.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of Pd-Au and Pd-Cu bimetallic catalysts supported on rutile TiO2, a phase known for its stability and influence on metal-support interactions. Catalytic testing will involve ethane oxidation in a aqueous-phase reactor under mild conditions (50-90°C, 10-30 bar) with H2 and O2 co-feeds to generate H2O2 in situ. The influence of dissolved chlorides (e.g., NaCl) on activity and selectivity will be systematically studied, drawing on pre-2026 insights into chloride effects on titania's oxidative properties. Advanced characterization (XPS, EPR, in-situ DRIFTS) will be used to probe catalyst surfaces and detect radical intermediates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed demonstration of extending the in-situ H2O2/radical mechanism from methane to ethane oxidation, establishing a potential general platform for alkane functionalization. It will deliver fundamental insights into how catalyst composition (bimetallic, rutile support) and reaction medium (chloride ions) govern selectivity between C-C bond cleavage and preservation during radical oxidation, contributing to catalyst design principles for complex alkane feedstocks.
proposals.novelty: While the radical pathway for methane is known, its application to ethane is non-trivial due to competing C-C bond scission risks; the novelty lies in deliberately applying this mechanism to a molecule with a C-C bond. Furthermore, combining Hutchings' expertise in in-situ H2O2 generation with the specific influence of chlorides—inspired by his photocatalytic work—on a rutile-supported bimetallic system for ethane oxidation is a unique and unexplored synthesis of his group's pre-2026 research themes.

proposals.title: In-situ spectroscopic and kinetic elucidation of surface intermediates and radical pathways in the selective oxidation of methane over Pd-based catalysts using in-situ H2O2
proposals.motivation: The selective oxidation of methane to methanol using in-situ generated H2O2 is a promising route to valorize natural gas, but achieving high selectivity remains a major challenge. Pre-2026 work, including Hutchings' own studies, suggests that selectivity is governed by complex surface chemistry and the potential involvement of free radicals, which can lead to over-oxidation. A detailed mechanistic understanding of the surface-bound intermediates and the role of radical quenching steps is critically lacking, hindering rational catalyst design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the selectivity of methane oxidation over Pd-based bimetallic catalysts is controlled by the lifetime and nature of key surface-bound oxygenate intermediates (e.g., methoxy, hydroperoxymethyl) and that chloride ions, known to influence titania photocatalysis, can be used as selective radical quenchers to suppress over-oxidation pathways and enhance methanol yield.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of in-situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy (DRIFTS) and modulation excitation spectroscopy (MES) to identify and track the formation and consumption of surface intermediates under realistic reaction conditions. Transient kinetic analysis, including temporal analysis of products (TAP) and isotopic labeling (18O2, D2O), will be used to measure intrinsic rate constants and elucidate elementary steps. The influence of chloride anions, introduced via controlled doping or as dissolved salts, on intermediate stability and radical-mediated pathways will be systematically investigated using radical scavenger experiments coupled with electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct spectroscopic and kinetic evidence for the surface intermediates involved in the methane-to-methanol reaction with in-situ H2O2. It will establish a quantitative link between surface speciation, radical chemistry, and product selectivity, offering a fundamental framework for catalyst optimization. The findings will also reveal how halide modifiers, a known influence in oxidation catalysis, can be leveraged to control selectivity at a mechanistic level.
proposals.novelty: While in-situ H2O2 generation for methane oxidation is established, the direct, time-resolved observation of surface intermediates and the deliberate use of chloride as a mechanistic probe for radical quenching in this specific catalytic cycle are novel. The integration of MES-DRIFTS with transient kinetics and radical detection methods to deconvolute this complex reaction network represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Influence of Dilute Oxygen Sources on the Activity and Selectivity of Pd-Based Bimetallic Catalysts for the Selective Oxidation of Methane via In Situ H₂O₂ Synthesis
proposals.motivation: The selective oxidation of methane to methanol using in situ generated H₂O₂ is a promising route for valorizing natural gas, but current research predominantly uses pure O₂ as the oxidant source. For industrial viability, a process must function efficiently with air or O₂-diluted streams to eliminate the cost and hazard of pure O₂ separation. Understanding how the N₂ ballast and trace impurities in air influence catalyst performance is a critical, underexplored step towards practical application.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using dilute O₂ sources (e.g., air) will significantly alter the surface oxidation state and H₂O₂ synthesis efficiency of Pd-based bimetallic catalysts, thereby influencing the overall activity and selectivity for methane oxidation to methanol, with catalyst composition (e.g., Pd-Au, Pd-Sn on rutile TiO₂) playing a decisive role in mitigating any negative effects.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of Pd-based bimetallic catalysts (e.g., Pd-Au, Pd-Sn) supported on rutile TiO₂, a phase known for its stability. Catalyst activity and selectivity for methane to methanol will be tested in a fixed-bed reactor under identical conditions (temperature, pressure, CH₄/H₂ ratio) while systematically varying the O₂ source: pure O₂, O₂ diluted with N₂ (5-21% O₂), and synthetic air. Online gas chromatography will quantify products, while in-situ/operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy and diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy will probe the catalyst's oxidation state and surface intermediates under reaction conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fundamental structure-activity relationship for methane oxidation catalysts under industrially relevant, dilute O₂ atmospheres. It will provide critical data on whether high selectivity can be maintained with air, directly addressing a major economic hurdle for process scale-up. The study may also reveal new mechanistic insights into the role of N₂ or trace gases in the catalytic cycle.
proposals.novelty: While Hutchings' prior work optimized catalysts for in situ H₂O₂ synthesis with pure O₂, systematically decoupling and studying the effect of the O₂ concentration and diluent gas (N₂) on the coupled methane oxidation pathway is novel. Combining this with the investigation of dissolved chloride effects on related titania systems provides a unique, holistic view of impurity impacts on catalytic oxidation.

proposals.title: Operando XAS and DRIFTS Elucidating the Role of Dissolved Chlorides in Modulating Pd Oxidation States and Methane Oxidation Pathways over Au-Pd/TiO2 Catalysts
proposals.motivation: The selective oxidation of methane to methanol using in situ generated H2O2 represents a major challenge in catalysis. Pre-2026 work by Hutchings and others shows that bimetallic Au-Pd catalysts on TiO2 (rutile) are promising, but selectivity remains limited. Concurrently, studies on photocatalysis indicate that dissolved chlorides profoundly influence TiO2 surface properties and reaction pathways, yet their role in thermocatalytic methane oxidation is unexplored. Understanding this chloride effect at a mechanistic level is crucial for designing robust, selective catalysts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dissolved chloride ions, present as a common impurity in water, dynamically modulate the oxidation state of Pd sites and the formation of key surface intermediates (e.g., Pd-OOH, CH3O*) during the Au-Pd/TiO2-catalyzed methane oxidation with H2 and O2, thereby controlling the selectivity between methanol and over-oxidation products.
proposals.method: We will employ synchrotron-based operando X-ray Absorption Spectroscopy (XAS) at the Pd K-edge to monitor the oxidation state and local coordination of Pd in real-time during reaction at 50-100°C in a trickle-bed reactor cell. Simultaneously, operando Diffuse Reflectance Infrared Fourier Transform Spectroscopy (DRIFTS) will identify surface-bound intermediates (peroxo, methoxy, formate) on the catalyst. Experiments will systematically vary chloride concentration (0-1000 ppm as NaCl) in the aqueous phase, correlating spectroscopic signatures with catalytic performance metrics (CH4 conversion, CH3OH selectivity, H2O2 yield) measured by online GC and HPLC.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, operando spectroscopic evidence of how environmental chlorides influence the active site and mechanism of a high-profile methane oxidation catalyst. It will establish structure-activity-selectivity relationships under realistic conditions, moving beyond idealized systems. The findings will guide the rational design of catalysts tolerant to, or even leveraging, halide ions for improved selectivity.
proposals.novelty: While operando studies of methane oxidation exist, none have investigated the explicit role of dissolved chlorides—a known influencer of TiO2 photocatalysis—on the oxidation state dynamics of Pd in a thermocatalytic system for in situ H2O2 generation and utilization. This bridges two distinct themes in Hutchings' pre-2026 portfolio: selective methane oxidation and chloride effects on titania properties.

proposals.title: Inverse Catalysis: Selective Hydrogenation of Aldehydes to Alcohols Using Au-Pd/TiO2 Catalysts Designed for In Situ H2O2 Synthesis
proposals.motivation: The selective hydrogenation of aldehydes to alcohols is a critical step in fine chemical synthesis, often requiring tailored catalysts to avoid over-reduction. Concurrently, the Au-Pd/TiO2 system pioneered by Hutchings' group is highly optimized for the selective oxidation of methane via in situ H2O2 generation. Exploring this catalyst's performance in the inverse reduction reaction presents a fundamental test of its bifunctional character and could reveal new, versatile catalytic platforms derived from oxidation systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The Au-Pd alloy nanoparticles supported on rutile TiO2, which are highly effective for the in situ synthesis and use of H2O2 for selective oxidations, will also exhibit high activity and selectivity for the hydrogenation of aldehydes to alcohols due to the synergistic activation of H2 and the aldehyde carbonyl group on the bimetallic surface.
proposals.method: We will synthesize Au-Pd catalysts on rutile TiO2 supports using established deposition-precipitation methods. The hydrogenation of representative aldehydes (e.g., benzaldehyde, propanal) will be performed in a batch reactor under H2 pressure (1-10 bar) at moderate temperatures (50-120°C). Reaction kinetics and selectivity will be rigorously analyzed by GC-MS and compared against monometallic Au/TiO2 and Pd/TiO2 controls. The influence of chloride residues (from precursor salts) on catalytic activity and metal leaching will be systematically studied via ICP-MS and XPS to decouple electronic from structural effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the bifunctional reduction-oxidation capability of a well-known oxidation catalyst, directly linking two seemingly inverse reaction pathways. It will provide fundamental insights into how catalyst design for one function (H2O2 synthesis/use) translates to another (H2 activation), potentially simplifying catalyst discovery for tandem processes. The study will also clarify the role of chloride and TiO2 polymorph (rutile) in modulating hydrogenation performance.
proposals.novelty: While Au-Pd/TiO2 is extensively studied for oxidation, its application to the hydrogenation of aldehydes is unexplored. The novelty lies in deliberately using a catalyst optimized for an oxidation cycle to drive a reduction, testing the reversibility of surface processes and the true multifunctionality of the active sites.

proposals.title: Designing Phosphonate and Silane-Based Bifunctional Linkers for the Immobilisation of Pd-Based Bimetallic Catalysts in Selective Methane Oxidation
proposals.motivation: The immobilisation of homogeneous or colloidal catalysts, such as the Pd-based bimetallics used for in situ H2O2 generation and methane oxidation, often leads to a significant loss of activity and selectivity due to suboptimal interactions with the oxide support. Existing linker chemistries (e.g., simple silanes) can create rigid, poorly controlled local environments that distort the active site. Developing tailored linkers is crucial to translate the promising performance of solution-phase catalysts into practical, recyclable solid systems for selective oxidation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that by designing hybrid organic-inorganic linkers with tailored head-group coordination (e.g., phosphonic acids for metal binding) and flexible, functionalised backbones, we can immobilise Pd-based bimetallic nanoparticles on titania (rutile) supports while preserving the electronic and geometric properties essential for high activity and methanol selectivity in methane oxidation.
proposals.method: We will synthesise a library of bifunctional linkers featuring a phosphonic acid or carboxylate group for anchoring to rutile TiO2, connected via alkyl or polyether spacers to a second functional group (e.g., thiol, amine, pyridine) designed to bind Pd and a second metal (e.g., Au, Cu). Linkers will be grafted onto high-surface-area rutile, followed by controlled deposition of metal precursors and reduction. Materials will be characterised by DRIFTS, XPS, and STEM-EDX. Catalytic testing will involve the selective oxidation of methane using in situ generated H2O2, with performance benchmarked against the unsupported analogues and catalysts attached via conventional silane linkers.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a rational design principle for catalyst immobilisation linkers, directly linking linker molecular structure to preserved catalytic performance. It will yield a new class of robust, tethered bimetallic catalysts for selective methane oxidation, providing fundamental insights into how the support interface influences critical properties like metal oxidation state and nanoparticle morphology.
proposals.novelty: While phosphonate anchors for TiO2 are known, their deliberate integration with specific metal-binding termini and tunable spacer lengths to *preserve the delicate synergy of a pre-formed bimetallic catalyst* during heterogenisation is a novel and non-obvious strategy, moving beyond simple catalyst attachment to active site engineering via the linker.

proposals.title: Engineering Rutile-TiO2 Pore Architectures to Modulate Mass Transfer and Enantioselectivity in Palladium-Catalysed Asymmetric Oxidations
proposals.motivation: The selective oxidation of methane to oxygenates using in situ generated H2O2, pioneered by Hutchings' group, faces challenges of over-oxidation and selectivity control. Concurrently, the influence of support morphology, such as rutile titania, on catalytic properties is well-established. Extending this to chiral catalysis, where enantioselectivity is highly sensitive to local reactant concentrations and diffusion rates, presents a critical opportunity to enhance performance by rational support design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that precisely engineering the pore size distribution and connectivity of rutile-TiO2 supports will minimise mass transfer limitations for chiral reactants and products, thereby enhancing both activity and enantioselectivity in palladium-catalysed asymmetric oxidation reactions.
proposals.method: We will synthesise a series of rutile-TiO2 supports with controlled pore architectures (micro-, meso-, and hierarchical) using templating methods (e.g., polymer and surfactant templating). These will be characterised via N2 physisorption, SEM, and XRD. Palladium and a chiral modifier (e.g., a cinchona alkaloid derivative) will be deposited to create heterogeneous chiral catalysts. Their performance will be evaluated in a model asymmetric oxidation (e.g., ethyl pyruvate hydrogenation or a selective alkene epoxidation) under conditions relevant to prior methane oxidation work. Kinetic analysis and the Thiele modulus will be used to quantify mass transfer effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish clear structure-property relationships between support pore geometry and enantioselectivity in heterogeneous catalysis. It will provide a general design principle for chiral catalyst supports, moving beyond empirical optimisation. The findings could translate to more efficient catalytic processes for synthesising high-value chiral intermediates.
proposals.novelty: While pore engineering is known for achiral reactions, its systematic application to control enantioselectivity by managing chiral mass transfer is underexplored. Combining Hutchings' expertise in oxidation catalysis and support effects with the concept of chiral induction creates a novel intersection.

proposals.title: Enhancing the Stability of Immobilised Pd-Based Bimetallic Catalysts via Surface Chloride Modification for Selective Methane Oxidation
proposals.motivation: The in situ generation of H2O2 for selective methane oxidation is a promising route to methanol, but the immobilised Pd-based bimetallic catalysts required for practical application suffer from metal leaching, especially in aqueous reaction media. This leaching leads to deactivation, contamination of the product stream, and loss of expensive metals, undermining the economic and environmental viability of the process. Understanding and mitigating this leaching is critical for advancing catalytic methane valorisation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that modifying the rutile titania support surface with controlled amounts of chloride ions will create stronger, more stable binding sites for Pd and its bimetallic partners (e.g., Au, Cu), thereby significantly reducing metal leaching during the catalytic cycle and subsequent catalyst recycling.
proposals.method: We will synthesise a series of rutile TiO2 supports with varying surface chloride concentrations via controlled impregnation or co-precipitation methods. Pd and a secondary metal (e.g., Au) will be deposited via deposition-precipitation. Catalyst stability will be rigorously tested in a continuous-flow reactor for methane oxidation with in situ H2O2 generation, analysing leached metals in the effluent via ICP-MS. The influence of chloride on the metal-support interaction, oxidation state, and local structure will be probed using XPS, HAADF-STEM, and EXAFS, correlating these properties with leaching resistance.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct structure-property relationship between surface anion modification, metal-support bonding, and leaching resistance in aqueous-phase oxidation catalysis. It will provide a novel, rational strategy for designing robust immobilised catalysts, moving beyond trial-and-error immobilisation. The findings will be directly applicable to improving the longevity and recyclability of catalysts for selective oxidation and other demanding liquid-phase reactions.
proposals.novelty: While the detrimental effect of chlorides on photocatalytic titania is known, their deliberate use as a surface modifier to *enhance* the stability of precious metal catalysts on rutile supports for thermocatalytic oxidation is a novel and counter-intuitive approach. This bridges concepts from photocatalysis and thermocatalysis within Hutchings' established research on bimetallic systems and methane oxidation.

proposals.title: Rational Design of Palladium-Ligand Complexes for Rutile-TiO2 Supports: A First-Principles Approach to Tune Methane Oxidation Selectivity
proposals.motivation: Conventional catalyst design often focuses on the active metal, treating the support as a static, secondary component. However, Hutchings' recent work demonstrates that support properties (e.g., rutile phase, surface chlorides) profoundly influence catalytic activity and selectivity, as seen in methane-to-methanol oxidation and photocatalytic degradation. A systematic, inverse methodology—starting from a well-defined support's electronic and structural properties to design a compatible molecular catalyst system—is lacking but could unlock precise control over reaction pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: By using first-principles calculations to map the electronic and adsorption properties of a rutile-TiO2 support (including chloride-modified surfaces), we can computationally design and screen tailored palladium-ligand complexes that, when anchored, will optimize the in situ generation and utilization of H2O2 for the selective oxidation of methane to methanol.
proposals.method: We will employ Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations using the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) to characterize pristine and chloride-adsorbed rutile-TiO2(110) surfaces. Descriptors such as surface work function, Lewis acidity, and oxygen vacancy formation energy will be computed. These descriptors will then be used as constraints in a ligand-screening algorithm. A database of organic ligands (phosphines, N-heterocyclic carbenes, etc.) will be screened via molecular DFT (Gaussian 16) for their Pd-complexation energy and resultant frontier orbital alignment with the support's surface states. Promising candidate complexes will be evaluated for key reaction steps in the methane oxidation cycle.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, generalizable descriptor-based framework for the *ab initio* design of catalyst-support systems. It will provide specific, testable predictions for new Pd-ligand catalysts on rutile-TiO2, moving beyond trial-and-error. The findings will deepen understanding of how molecular-scale ligand engineering can modulate metal-support interactions for selective oxidation.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the classic design paradigm by starting from the support's properties to computationally build a compatible molecular catalyst, a strategy not yet applied to the design of complex ligand systems for heterogeneous methane oxidation. It uniquely integrates Hutchings' insights on chloride influence and in situ H2O2 synthesis into a predictive computational model.

proposals.title: Membrane-Immobilised Pd-Au Bimetallic Catalysts for the Integrated Synthesis and Separation of Methanol from Methane via In Situ H₂O₂
proposals.motivation: The selective oxidation of methane to methanol using in situ generated H₂O₂ is a promising route for gas valorisation, but faces challenges in product separation and catalyst recovery, limiting process intensification. Microstructured devices offer enhanced mass transfer, but integrating catalytic reaction with selective product removal in a single, scalable unit remains a key hurdle. This work addresses the need for compact, efficient systems for distributed chemical synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: That incorporating Pd-Au bimetallic nanoparticles, known for in situ H₂O₂ synthesis, into a thin, hydrophilic polymer membrane (e.g., polyvinyl alcohol or Nafion) will simultaneously catalyse methane oxidation and selectively permeate the polar methanol product, thereby driving equilibrium conversion and enabling continuous operation in a microfluidic device.
proposals.method: Catalyst synthesis will follow established colloidal methods to deposit Pd-Au nanoparticles (1-3 nm) onto rutile TiO₂ nanorods, leveraging known promotional effects. These nanocomposites will be uniformly dispersed into casting solutions of selective polymer matrices and fabricated into thin (<5 µm) composite membranes on porous ceramic supports. The membranes will be integrated into a custom microfluidic reactor, and performance will be evaluated under continuous flow of CH₄, O₂, and H₂, analysing methanol yield and selectivity in the permeate stream versus the retentate. The influence of dissolved chlorides (e.g., NaCl) in any aqueous phase on both catalytic activity and membrane swelling/permeability will be systematically studied.
proposals.contribution: This will demonstrate a novel process-intensified unit operation combining reaction and separation for methane oxidation, moving beyond batch or sequential systems. It will provide fundamental insights into how nanoscale catalyst environment and confinement within a selective membrane influence catalytic cycles and product diffusion. The work will deliver a prototype for modular, continuous methanol synthesis.
proposals.novelty: While catalytic membranes exist, the specific integration of Hutchings' pre-optimised Pd-Au/TiO₂ catalysts for in situ H₂O₂ generation into a tailored membrane for *combined* synthesis and immediate separation of methanol in microdevice format is unexplored. The study of chloride effects, known to influence titania photocatalysis, on this novel catalytic membrane system adds a further unique dimension.

proposals.title: Solvent-Modulated Kinetics and Product Management in the Direct Oxidation of Methane to Methanol via In Situ H₂O₂ Synthesis
proposals.motivation: The direct, selective oxidation of methane to methanol remains a grand challenge in catalysis, with solvent-free conditions often pursued to avoid over-oxidation. However, these systems can suffer from poor methanol extraction and catalyst deactivation. Pre-2026 work by Hutchings and others highlights the critical influence of reaction environment, including the detrimental role of chlorides in photocatalytic systems, suggesting that deliberate solvent choice could be a powerful, underexplored lever to control reactivity and separation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that introducing a carefully selected polar solvent (e.g., water, methanol, or aqueous acids) will modulate the local concentration of reactive oxygen species and methanol product, thereby enhancing overall methanol yield by improving kinetics of in situ H₂O₂ generation and facilitating immediate product removal from the catalyst surface.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of Pd-based bimetallic catalysts (e.g., Pd-Au, Pd-Fe) supported on rutile TiO₂, chosen for its established properties in oxidation. These will be tested in a high-pressure batch reactor for methane oxidation using H₂ and O₂ for in situ H₂O₂ synthesis. The key variable will be the introduction of solvents (water, dilute sulfuric acid, methanol) at controlled volumes. Reaction kinetics will be monitored via online mass spectrometry and GC, with post-reaction analysis of the liquid phase and catalyst (XRD, XPS) to assess methanol selectivity, leaching, and the influence of solvent on chloride impurity effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for managing product inhibition in direct methane oxidation, moving beyond the strict 'solvent-free' dogma. It will provide a fundamental understanding of how solvents influence the reaction network of in situ H₂O₂ generation and methane activation, offering a practical strategy to improve process continuity and catalyst lifetime.
proposals.novelty: While solvent-free conditions are typically seen as essential to prevent over-oxidation, this proposal inverts that logic by deliberately using a solvent as a kinetic and separation modulator, integrating concepts from Hutchings' work on methane oxidation and the environmental influence of chlorides on titania properties.

proposals.title: Direct Aerobic Oxidation of Ethylbenzene and Xylenes to Acetophenone and Toluic Acids using Au-Pd/TiO2 Catalysts with In Situ H2O2 Generation
proposals.motivation: The selective oxidation of alkyl aromatic hydrocarbons to valuable oxygenated intermediates (e.g., acetophenone, toluic acids) is a critical industrial process, traditionally reliant on stoichiometric oxidants or harsh conditions, generating significant waste. Building on Hutchings' group's pre-2026 breakthroughs in methane-to-methanol oxidation using in situ H2O2 over Au-Pd catalysts, extending this green catalytic approach to liquid-phase alkyl aromatics offers a route to more sustainable fine chemical synthesis. The influence of chloride ions, a known catalyst modifier and potential poison, on this system remains an important, underexplored variable given its relevance to industrial feedstocks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that supported Au-Pd bimetallic nanoparticles, particularly on rutile TiO2, will catalyze the selective aerobic oxidation of ethylbenzene and xylenes to their corresponding esters/acids via in situ generated H2O2 as a key oxidant, and that controlled addition of chloride ions will modulate selectivity by influencing the oxidation state and surface properties of the catalyst.
proposals.method: We will synthesize Au-Pd (1:1 molar ratio) catalysts via sol-immobilization on rutile TiO2, characterized by TEM, XPS, and XRD. Catalytic testing will involve batch reactions in water/acetonitrile solvent under O2 pressure (10-30 bar) at 50-90°C, analyzing products via GC-MS and HPLC. The influence of chloride will be systematically studied by adding controlled amounts of HCl or NaCl to the reaction medium. Parallel experiments will quantify in situ H2O2 formation using iodometric titration to correlate its concentration with substrate conversion and product selectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, greener catalytic route for alkyl aromatic oxidation, directly translating a concept proven for methane to more complex, liquid substrates. It will provide fundamental insights into the role of in situ H2O2 in liquid-phase oxidations and generate a detailed understanding of how chloride ions, often present as impurities, influence activity and selectivity in noble metal-catalyzed aerobic oxidations.
proposals.novelty: While Au-Pd catalysts are known for alcohol oxidation and methane activation, their application to the direct oxidation of ethylbenzene and xylenes using the in situ H2O2 pathway is unexplored. The deliberate investigation of chloride as a tunable parameter in this context, connecting to the group's prior work on chlorides in photocatalytic systems, introduces a novel dimension for catalyst optimization.

proposals.title: Machine Learning-Guided Design of Au-Pd Alloy Catalysts for the Selective Oxidation of Methane via In Situ H2O2 Synthesis
proposals.motivation: The selective oxidation of methane to methanol using in situ generated H2O2 is a critical challenge in catalysis, offering a potential route to valorize natural gas. However, the activity and selectivity of Au-Pd bimetallic catalysts, a leading class for this reaction, are highly sensitive to alloy structure, composition, and surface properties, making empirical optimization costly and slow. A predictive framework is needed to navigate this complex design space efficiently.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that machine learning models trained on a combination of experimental catalytic data and computed electronic/geometric descriptors can accurately predict the catalytic performance (activity and methanol selectivity) of Au-Pd alloy structures, enabling the identification of optimal compositions and morphologies not accessible through traditional trial-and-error.
proposals.method: We will curate a dataset from published literature (pre-2026) and our own experimental work on Au-Pd catalysts for methane oxidation, including features such as alloy composition, particle size, support identity, and reaction conditions. Catalytic performance labels will be methane conversion and methanol selectivity. We will generate additional electronic structure descriptors (e.g., d-band center, coordination numbers) using Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations on model clusters. Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), which effectively model atomic connectivity, will be the primary architecture to learn structure-property relationships. Model predictions will guide the synthesis and testing of new catalyst candidates.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated ML model for Au-Pd catalyst design, providing new fundamental insights into the key descriptors governing performance in selective methane oxidation. It will directly yield novel, high-performance catalyst formulations, accelerating the development of this transformative technology. The methodology will establish a blueprint for data-driven catalyst discovery in other complex oxidation systems.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been applied to heterogeneous catalysis, its targeted integration with the specific, complex reaction network of in situ H2O2 synthesis for methane oxidation—combining experimental data with DFT-derived descriptors for bimetallic alloys—is unprecedented. This approach moves beyond simple property prediction to actively guide the discovery of catalysts for a reaction where traditional design principles are insufficient.

proposals.title: Photocatalytic Activation of Pd-Au/TiO2 for the Selective Oxidation of Methane to Methanol via In Situ H2O2 Synthesis
proposals.motivation: The direct, selective oxidation of methane to methanol remains a grand challenge in catalysis, with existing thermal routes often suffering from over-oxidation. Hutchings' recent work demonstrates a promising pathway using in situ generated H2O2 over Pd-based bimetallic catalysts. Separately, the influence of chloride ions on TiO2 photocatalysis is known, but its effect on catalytic methane oxidation systems is unexplored. Integrating photocatalysis could lower the energy barrier for H2O2 generation and methane activation, potentially improving selectivity and yield under milder conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that UV-visible light irradiation of a Pd-Au bimetallic catalyst supported on rutile TiO2 will enhance the in situ generation of H2O2 and the subsequent activation of C-H bonds in methane, leading to a higher yield of methanol at lower temperatures compared to the purely thermal reaction. We further hypothesize that the presence of controlled amounts of dissolved chlorides will modulate the surface properties of the TiO2 support, influencing charge carrier dynamics and ultimately the photocatalytic activity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of Pd-Au bimetallic catalysts supported on commercial and lab-synthesized rutile TiO2. Catalytic testing will be performed in a custom-built, high-pressure photoreactor equipped with a UV-Vis light source (e.g., 365 nm LED or Xe lamp with appropriate filters). The reaction will be studied under varying conditions of light intensity, temperature (25-100°C), pressure (10-30 bar CH4/O2/H2), and with the addition of controlled concentrations of NaCl to the aqueous reaction medium. Activity will be assessed by quantifying methanol yield and H2O2 concentration via HPLC and iodometric titration, respectively. Catalyst characterization (XRD, XPS, UV-Vis DRS, TEM) will link structural and electronic properties to performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, hybrid photocatalytic-thermocatalytic pathway for selective methane oxidation, potentially revealing synergistic effects between light and heat. It will provide fundamental insights into how chloride ions, common in many water sources, influence the photocatalytic properties of TiO2 in a complex, multi-step catalytic cycle. The findings could guide the design of next-generation catalysts for solar-driven upgrading of natural gas.
proposals.novelty: While photocatalytic methane oxidation and thermal H2O2-assisted oxidation are known separately, the direct application of light to activate Hutchings' established Pd-Au/TiO2 system for in situ H2O2 synthesis and methane oxidation is novel. The deliberate investigation of chloride's influence bridges two distinct areas of the researcher's recent work, creating a unique, unexplored intersection.

proposals.title: Selective Hydrogenation of Benzyl Benzoate to Toluene via Direct C–O Bond Cleavage Using Au-Pd/TiO2 Catalysts
proposals.motivation: The selective hydrogenation of esters to deoxygenated hydrocarbons is a challenging but valuable transformation for biomass valorization and chemical synthesis. Pre-2026, Hutchings' group has pioneered Au-Pd catalysts for selective oxidations, including methane-to-methanol via in situ H2O2. This proposal inverts that logic, applying similar bimetallic formulations to a demanding reduction. Understanding how chloride residues (from catalyst synthesis or feeds) influence the metal-support interaction and activity, as seen in related photocatalytic studies, provides a crucial secondary variable.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Au-Pd nanoparticles supported on rutile TiO2, analogous to those used for selective oxidations, will catalyze the direct hydrogenolysis of benzyl benzoate to toluene, and that controlled chloride addition will modulate metal electronic properties to enhance selectivity by suppressing over-reduction.
proposals.method: We will synthesize Au-Pd bimetallic catalysts (1-5 wt% total metal, varying Au:Pd ratios) on rutile TiO2 via wet impregnation and sol-immobilization, with and without chloride precursors (e.g., HAuCl4, PdCl2). Catalytic testing will be performed in a fixed-bed reactor under H2 pressure (10-30 bar, 150-250°C). Reaction products will be analyzed by GC-MS and GC-FID. Catalyst characterization will involve XRD, TEM, XPS, and CO-DRIFTS to correlate chloride influence on particle size, oxidation state, and surface acidity with activity and selectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel application of Hutchings' signature Au-Pd catalysts for ester hydrogenolysis, directly linking oxidation and reduction catalysis. It will provide fundamental insights into the role of chloride modifiers in tuning bimetallic catalysts for C–O bond activation, extending concepts from photocatalytic degradation to thermocatalysis. The findings could open a new route for aromatic chemical production from esterified platforms.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a catalyst system optimized for C–H oxidation with H2O2 to the inverse problem of C–O hydrogenolysis with H2, and in deliberately using chloride as a promoter rather than a poison, based on pre-2026 observations of its influence on titania-supported metal properties.

proposals.title: Computational Elucidation of Chloride Ion Influence on Aqueous-Phase Methane Oxidation Pathways over Pd-TiO2 Catalysts
proposals.motivation: The selective oxidation of methane to methanol using in situ generated H2O2 in aqueous media is a major pre-2026 challenge. Hutchings' recent experimental work highlights the significant but poorly understood influence of dissolved chlorides on catalytic activity and selectivity, particularly for palladium on titania (rutile) systems. A mechanistic understanding of how chloride ions modulate reaction pathways at the atomic level is crucial for rational catalyst design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chloride ions in the aqueous phase selectively stabilize key Pd-OOH and Pd-OCH3 intermediates via explicit solvation and ion-pairing, thereby altering the activation barriers for C-H scission and methanol desorption, which explains the experimentally observed changes in activity and selectivity.
proposals.method: We will employ density functional theory (DFT) using the PBE functional with Grimme's D3 dispersion correction, as implemented in CP2K, to model explicit aqueous environments. The solvated Pd cluster on a rutile TiO2(110) slab will be simulated using ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) to sample chloride adsorption configurations. Free energy profiles for the methane-to-methanol pathway will be computed using constrained molecular dynamics and thermodynamic integration, comparing systems with and without explicit chloride ions and counter-ions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first atomistic mechanism for chloride promotion/inhibition in aqueous-phase methane oxidation, directly linking to Hutchings' experimental findings. It will establish design principles for tuning catalyst performance via electrolyte engineering, moving beyond traditional gas-phase catalyst models to realistic liquid-phase environments.
proposals.novelty: While DFT studies of methane oxidation exist, none have explicitly modeled the dynamic role of dissolved chloride ions in the aqueous phase on Pd-TiO2 interfaces. This integrates Hutchings' expertise in catalytic oxidation, chlorides, and titania properties into a novel computational framework focused on solvated ion-catalyst interactions.

ideas.title: Support-Dependent Aggregation States of Pre-Catalytic Metal Species
ideas.description: This research direction combines the inversion of solution aggregation assumptions with comparative support analysis. It posits that the nature of the oxide support (e.g., titania vs. ceria) actively dictates the degree of aggregation of metal precursor species during deposition from solution, challenging the view of aggregation as a purely solution-phase property. By applying advanced microscopy to multiple supports, the work will correlate specific support properties (redox activity, surface hydroxyl density, point of zero charge) with the resulting aggregation states of the deposited metal species. This creates a new paradigm for rationally selecting supports to engineer pre-catalytic ensembles with targeted aggregation for specific reactions.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Determination of Active Site Nuclearity via Hypothesis-Neutral Speciation and Kinetics
ideas.description: This redesigned study systematically determines the nuclearity of the true catalytic active species without an initial bias. It combines comprehensive spectroscopic speciation analysis under catalytic conditions with the synthesis of discrete nuclearity-defined model complexes to serve as spectroscopic and mechanistic benchmarks. Kinetic and mechanistic studies, including order-in-catalyst experiments and selective poisoning experiments, are then used to directly link observed species to catalytic turnover. This approach rigorously distinguishes between dinuclear active sites, mononuclear sites with secondary metal interactions, and higher-order clusters, delivering a definitive conclusion on active site structure.

ideas.title: High-Throughput Screening of Cluster Catalysts for Selective Oxidation Using a Biochemical Assay Analogy

The analogous problem—rapidly testing the activity of many compounds (e.g., potential drugs) against a specific enzymatic reaction—is solved using high-throughput screening (HTS) with optical assays. We adapt this solution to the catalysis context. The research idea is to implement a high-throughput screening platform to evaluate if the identified active clusters catalyze selective oxidation reactions beyond CO oxidation, such as alcohol or amine oxidation. This involves using chromogenic or fluorogenic probe molecules for these reactions in a multi-well plate format, allowing for the parallel, rapid optical detection of catalytic activity across a broad substrate panel to efficiently determine the functional scope and selectivity of the clusters.

ideas.title: Activity Mapping of Atomically Precise, Support-Immobilized Clusters via Synthetic and Operando Characterization Funnels
ideas.description: This research overcomes attribution ambiguity by constructing catalysts from pre-formed, ligand-capped metal clusters of defined nuclearity and geometry, which are then grafted onto inert oxide supports. A rigorous, two-stage characterization funnel first verifies the structural integrity of the deposited clusters and the absence of bilayer phases ex situ. Subsequently, operando spectroscopic measurements under reaction conditions directly link the persistent electronic and geometric structure of the specific cluster morphology to the catalytic turnover, providing an unambiguous structure-activity relationship for non-bilayer active sites.

ideas.title: Dimer-to-aggregate deactivation pathways: scaling laws for dinuclear catalyst degradation
ideas.description: This research program investigates catalyst deactivation as a scaling problem specifically for systems where dinuclear complexes are the dominant active species. It seeks to establish how the aggregation of these essential dinuclear intermediates into larger, inactive clusters scales with time and temperature, directly linking molecular identity to deactivation kinetics. The core hypothesis is that the stability and aggregation propensity of the dinuclear motif itself dictates the functional lifetime of the catalyst. This enables a rational design strategy to inhibit deactivation by engineering ligands or supports that selectively destabilize the transition state for dimer-dimer aggregation, while preserving the catalytically crucial dinuclear structure.

ideas.title: Deconvoluting physical and chemical scaling in catalyst deactivation pathways
ideas.description: This research moves beyond correlating aggregate size with activity loss to mechanistically separate the scaling of physical particle growth from the scaling of chemical state evolution during deactivation. By employing operando characterization synchronized with activity measurements, the project will map how the distribution of active sites and poisoned surfaces changes as a function of time-on-stream and temperature, not merely as a function of cluster size. This approach identifies the dominant deactivation pathway (e.g., sintering vs. site poisoning vs. phase transformation) under specific conditions, enabling the design of targeted stabilization strategies based on the actual scaling laws of the critical failure mode.

ideas.title: Deconvoluting Active Sites: A Quantitative Framework to Distinguish Single-Atom, Cluster, and Interface Contributions in Complex Catalysts
ideas.description: This redesigned approach moves beyond the binary spectator/active claim to develop a quantitative, falsifiable methodology for assigning catalytic activity. It employs isotopically labeled precursor molecules to synthesize model catalysts with controlled populations of single atoms and defined small clusters (M1, M2, M3-5, M6-10) on inert, atomically flat supports. Activity is measured under operando conditions while simultaneously using advanced spectroscopic and microscopic techniques (e.g., time-resolved XAFS, high-pressure STEM-EDS) to track the state and local environment of each tagged species. The resulting data feeds into a microkinetic model that explicitly includes potential active sites of different nuclearity, allowing the experimental turnover frequencies to be partitioned and the true active species to be identified based on predictive power, not just correlation.

ideas.title: Operando Grand-Canonical AIMD Modeling of Bilayer Cluster Catalysis
ideas.description: This approach employs grand-canonical density functional theory (GC-DFT) coupled with ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) to model the catalytic activity of bilayer clusters under realistic electrochemical conditions. The simulations include explicit solvent molecules and electrolyte ions, allowing the cluster structure and adsorbates to evolve dynamically at the solid-liquid interface. By controlling the electron chemical potential, the model directly simulates the system at a specified electrode potential, enabling the calculation of potential-dependent reaction pathways and free energy barriers. This integrated computational framework is designed to bridge the materials gap by predicting activity trends that account for solvation, dynamics, and electrochemical polarization.

ideas.title: Catalytic Activity from Isolated Single-Atom and Sub-Nanometer Clusters on Engineered Defect Sites
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a bilayer structure is essential for catalytic activity by drawing an analogy from semiconductor doping in solid-state physics. In that field, introducing isolated impurity atoms at specific lattice sites, rather than a continuous layer, fundamentally changes a material's properties. The adapted research idea proposes to engineer catalyst supports with precise defect sites to trap and stabilize isolated single atoms or sub-nanometer clusters of the active species. The catalytic activity is hypothesized to emerge from the specific electronic interaction at these isolated sites, making the bilayer morphology non-essential and opening pathways to design catalysts based on atomic-scale engineering of the support.

ideas.title: Gram-scale synthesis of bilayer catalysts via biomimetic self-assembly and confinement
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from structural biology—the use of lipidic matrices to grow large, high-quality membrane protein crystals—to the challenge of gram-scale synthesis of pure bilayer catalysts. The analogous problem of controlling macromolecular assembly within a bilayer-like environment is solved using lipidic cubic phase (LCP) or bicelle confinement. The adapted idea proposes employing a tunable, self-assembled soft mesophase (e.g., from surfactants or block copolymers) as a biomimetic confining template. This matrix will act as a synthetic 'bilayer mimic' during catalyst precursor deposition and reduction, directing the preferential, scalable synthesis of the active bilayer cluster morphology by providing the correct geometric and chemical environment for its formation, thereby suppressing competing pathways that lead to 3D nanoparticles.

ideas.title: Operando microfluidic nanoreactor for single-cluster electrocatalysis
ideas.description: This system combines nanofabricated electrode arrays for isolating individual bilayer clusters with a sealed microfluidic channel for controlled electrolyte delivery. It enables the application of a precise electrochemical potential while flowing reactants past the single cluster, allowing for the direct measurement of Faradaic current and turnover frequency. In-situ optical or vibrational spectroscopy (e.g., SERS, fluorescence) integrated into the flow cell correlates catalytic activity with the cluster's structural or electronic state in real time. This approach directly measures the intrinsic activity of a single catalytic entity under realistic, dynamic reaction conditions.

ideas.title: Correlative in situ ETEM and APT for 3D atomic-scale analysis of dynamic cluster evolution
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the static limitation of standalone APT by first using in situ environmental TEM to observe and record the dynamic behavior of catalyst clusters (e.g., sintering, redispersion, shape change) under realistic gas and temperature conditions. Specific regions of interest identified during these dynamic experiments are then precisely excised and analyzed using atom probe tomography to obtain their definitive 3D atomic composition and atomic-scale structure. The correlative workflow, enabled by fiducial markers, directly links a dynamic process to its final atomic-scale state. This provides unprecedented insight into the structure-composition relationships that govern catalyst stability and activity evolution under operando conditions.

ideas.title: Spectator Clusters: A 'Dark Matter' Problem in Catalysis
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that identified metal clusters are the active sites in catalysis, proposing they may be spectators or byproducts. Drawing an analogy from cosmology's solution to the dark matter problem—where the unseen component is mapped by its effects on the visible—we adapt this to catalysis. The adapted research idea is to treat prominent clusters as the 'visible matter' and meticulously map the true catalytic 'dark matter' (single atoms or interfaces) by designing experiments that spatially and temporally decouple activity signals from specific structural features. Using advanced in-situ spectroscopy and microscopy, the goal is to correlate activity hot spots with underlying atomic structure with unprecedented precision, thereby inferring the true active sites from their 'catalytic effects' rather than from their structural prominence.

ideas.title: Reaction-Specific Optimal Size: A Phase Diagram Approach
ideas.description: The original idea challenges the assumption that a ~0.5 nm cluster size threshold is universally optimal for catalysis. Drawing an analogy from materials science, where optimal grain size is condition-dependent and mapped via phase diagrams, we adapt this solution. The research idea is to construct multi-parameter 'catalytic performance phase diagrams' that plot optimal cluster size against key reaction variables (temperature, pressure, reactant partial pressures, support material). This framework would formally demonstrate that the optimal size is not a fixed number but a variable that migrates across the diagram's axes, making the condition-specific nature of size-activity relationships explicit and predictable.

ideas.title: Design catalysts with controlled cluster spacing using DNA origami patterning
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from DNA nanotechnology to solve the catalyst design problem. In DNA origami, functional components are positioned with nanometer precision via programmed base-pairing. The analogous solution is to use a designed DNA origami nanostructure as a molecular template, where staple strands modified with metal-coordinating ligands create a precise 2D array of nucleation sites. By binding metal precursors exclusively to these sites and subsequently converting the bio-template into a durable inorganic support, one can synthesize a catalyst where the spatial distribution, density, and therefore the cooperative or isolation effects between active metal clusters are controlled at the nanoscale. This approach directly addresses the SCALING_DUAL dimension by enabling the design of catalyst supports with patterned sites from the bottom-up.

ideas.title: Applying Population Dynamics Models to Predict Catalyst Cluster Aggregation
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from population biology in ecology. The analogous problem is modeling how populations of organisms or particles aggregate and change size distribution over time, classically solved using Smoluchowski coagulation equations. We adapt this solution to heterogeneous catalysis. The research will investigate catalyst deactivation by modeling metal or oxide clusters on a support as a dynamic population. The rate of cluster-cluster aggregation will be defined by a kernel incorporating temperature-dependent mobility and interaction potentials. By solving these adapted population dynamics equations, the study aims to predict the scaling laws governing how the cluster size distribution evolves, leading to a quantifiable and predictable loss of catalytic activity as a function of time and temperature, framing deactivation fundamentally as a scaling problem in cluster population dynamics.

ideas.title: Computationally-Guided Experimental Mapping of Solvent-Dependent Cluster Stability and Dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction integrates computational and experimental approaches to fundamentally understand how solvent choice dictates the stability and activity of molecular bilayer clusters. Density functional theory (DFT) will be used to model the electronic structure of the clusters and predict their interaction energies, solvation shells, and stability in a diverse range of non-standard solvents (e.g., ionic liquids, deep eutectic solvents, fluorinated aromatics). These computational predictions will then directly inform the selection of specific solvents for targeted experimental validation using NMR spectroscopy and freezing point depression studies. The resulting experimental data will, in turn, refine the computational models, creating a synergistic cycle that allows for the rational design of solvent matrices to precisely tune cluster properties for specific applications.

ideas.title: Probing Solution Aggregates via Cryo-Electron Microscopy and Light Scattering
ideas.description: This idea challenges the monomeric assumption in solution chemistry by drawing an analogy from structural biology, where techniques like cryo-EM and MALS revealed the prevalence and importance of protein oligomers and aggregates. The analogous solution was to directly visualize and quantify solution species without preconceived models. Adapted to chemical research, this means employing cryo-EM to image vitrified solutions of synthetic molecules, catalysts, or reactants to identify aggregated states, complemented by in-situ light scattering to monitor aggregation sizes and distributions under relevant conditions. This shift from assuming monomers to empirically mapping the aggregation landscape could redefine understanding of reaction mechanisms, catalytic active sites, and material nucleation pathways.

ideas.title: Decoupling Geometric and Electronic Effects via Site-Patterned Supports for Morphology-Diverse Sub-nm Clusters
ideas.description: This research direction proposes creating supports with atomically-precise, patterned anchoring sites to synthesize and stabilize a library of sub-nanometer clusters with distinct, well-defined morphologies (e.g., rings, chains, 3D shapes) that are not bilayer structures. The patterned support actively controls the inter-cluster spacing to prevent sintering while also presenting a uniform local electronic environment for each site, ensuring that differences in activity are primarily due to the cluster's geometric shape and nuclearity. This approach decouples the geometric effect of cluster morphology from confounding factors like particle size distributions and uncontrolled support interactions. The goal is to establish fundamental structure-activity relationships that reveal which specific cluster morphologies, enabled by this tailored synthesis platform, are optimal for given catalytic transformations.

ideas.title: Adapting Cryo-EM Particle Picking and Classification for Atomic Counting in Gold Clusters
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the core computational methodology from cryo-electron microscopy (cryo-EM) to solve the analogous problem of quantifying the precise atomic count in active ~10-atom gold bilayer clusters. In cryo-EM, the 3D atomic structure of proteins is solved by computationally picking, classifying, and averaging millions of noisy 2D particle images. We propose to apply this analogous solution to advanced microscopy data (e.g., aberration-corrected STEM) of gold clusters on supports. An adapted computational pipeline will be developed to automatically identify and isolate images of individual clusters from time-series or multi-frame datasets, perform 2D classification to group clusters by identical size and orientation, and then align and average all images within a class. This signal averaging will yield a noise-suppressed, ultra-high signal-to-noise ratio image where the position of each gold atom becomes unambiguous, thereby allowing exact atomic enumeration and precise structural modeling of the active catalytic sites. The key innovation is the transfer of computational single-particle analysis techniques from structural biology to the domain of inorganic cluster catalysis.

ideas.title: Adapting Polymer Rheology Methods to Contrast Mono- and Dinuclear Complex Dynamics
ideas.description: This research idea adapts solution strategies from polymer rheology to systematically contrast mononuclear and dinuclear complexes. In polymer physics, the distinct behaviors of linear versus branched polymers are resolved by coupling macroscopic rheological measurements (viscosity, moduli) with microscopic probes of chain dynamics. Adapted here, mononuclear complexes are analogous to monomeric units, while dinuclear complexes are treated as dimers with potential electronic or magnetic coupling acting as 'cross-links'. The adapted solution involves a paired experimental approach: using variable-temperature NMR, EPR, and optical spectroscopy to probe local metal center dynamics (the 'monomer motion'), while simultaneously employing AC magnetic susceptibility, dielectric spectroscopy, and conductivity measurements to detect and quantify cooperative inter-center phenomena (the 'viscoelastic' response). This data will be rigorously correlated with solid-state structural insights from X-ray diffraction and computational modeling, providing a unified framework to decouple single-site properties from multi-center interactions in both solution and solid states.

ideas.title: Adapting Cryo-ET Subtomogram Averaging to APT for Enhanced 3D Compositional Mapping
ideas.description: The original idea is to employ atom probe tomography to get a 3D atomic-scale composition of clusters and support. Drawing an analogy from structural biology, specifically cryo-electron tomography (cryo-ET), where the 'subtomogram averaging' technique is used to overcome noise and reveal high-fidelity 3D structure. In cryo-ET, many copies of a macromolecule are extracted, aligned, and averaged. This concept is adapted for APT data analysis. Instead of a single reconstruction, multiple 3D compositional maps of similar nanoclusters are extracted from the APT volume. These maps are computationally aligned based on their morphology and/or known crystallography, and their compositional data (ion counts per voxel) are averaged. This process mitigates the effects of local reconstruction artifacts, trajectory aberrations, and detection noise inherent in APT, producing a statistically robust, averaged 3D compositional map that more accurately represents the true atomic distribution within the clusters and at the cluster-support interface.

ideas.title: Apply cryo-EM with vitrification and low-dose imaging to preserve metal cluster structure
ideas.description: This idea adapts the established cryo-EM solution from structural biology, where imaging radiation-sensitive, solvated biological complexes is a central challenge. The analogous solution involves rapid vitrification to preserve native state, followed by low-electron-dose imaging and computational single-particle analysis to build a high-resolution 3D model from many noisy 2D projections. Adapted to cluster science, the research would involve preparing metal or molecular clusters in solution, vitrifying them on cryo-EM grids to preserve their solution-phase structure and prevent beam-induced aggregation or rearrangement. Using a direct electron detector, large datasets of low-dose micrographs would be collected. Subsequent computational processing—particle picking, classification, and 3D reconstruction—would then reveal the preserved 3D architecture of the clusters without the damage typically caused by traditional TEM imaging.

ideas.title: Integrated microfluidic rapid-mixing and validation for native-state cryo-EM of fragile clusters
ideas.description: This redesigned approach addresses sample preparation artifacts by using a microfluidic chip to mix cluster components and prepare cryo-EM grids in a single, rapid, and controlled process. The chip minimizes exposure to destructive interfaces and allows reaction intermediates to be trapped by vitrification. An inline solution-phase scattering measurement validates the cluster structure in liquid immediately before freezing, confirming fidelity. This integrated system ensures the imaged cryo-preserved clusters accurately represent their functional, solution-phase architectures.

ideas.title: Mapping Solvation Landscapes via a Solvent Property Matrix
ideas.description: This idea adapts the ecological approach of mapping species distributions across environmental gradients to the study of solvation. In biogeography, systematic sampling across defined gradients (like temperature and moisture) reveals habitat suitability. Analogously, we propose defining a key solvent property space (e.g., using parameters like dielectric constant, Kamlet-Taft parameters, etc.) and selecting a representative matrix of non-standard solvents that span this space. Performing coordinated NMR and freezing point studies across this matrix will generate a systematic dataset. This allows the construction of a predictive 'solvation landscape' model, revealing how solution behavior shifts as a function of fundamental solvent properties, moving beyond qualitative observations to a quantitative, mapping-based framework.

ideas.title: Physics-Grounded Generative Models for Interpretable Cluster Analysis in STEM
ideas.description: This redesigned approach combines deep learning segmentation with a physics-constrained generative adversarial network (GAN) or diffusion model to move beyond mere classification. The system first identifies candidate cluster regions in STEM data, then generates a synthetic, atomically-resolved reconstruction of each region that is consistent with both the image data and fundamental materials constraints. The final output is a human-interpretable report detailing each cluster's proposed structure, chemical signature, and defect characteristics, enabling direct physical validation and hypothesis testing.

ideas.title: Adapting Astronomical Galaxy Cluster Detection for STEM Nanocluster Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from astronomy, where machine learning, specifically deep convolutional neural networks, is used to automatically detect and classify galaxy clusters in massive celestial image surveys. The analogous problem in materials science is the identification of nanoscale clusters (e.g., precipitates, defects, or nanoparticles) within large STEM datasets. The adapted research idea involves developing a CNN-based analysis pipeline specifically for STEM imaging. The model would be trained on a combination of physically realistic simulated microstructures and a manually curated ground-truth dataset of labeled cluster types. This approach would automate the detection, segmentation, and morphological classification of clusters, dramatically accelerating the analysis of material microstructure-property relationships from large-scale experimental or computational STEM datasets, much as it has for cataloging cosmic structures.
